chapters.title: Learning vocabulary
chapters.summary: While English boasts a vast vocabulary, most native speakers use only a limited number of words daily. This unit explains that learning vocabulary means noticing usage, grammar, pronunciation, register, and effective ways to remember and expand new words.
points: Knowing the definition of a word isn't enough; you also need to understand its usage.
Consider a word's grammar, pronunciation, and register (formal, informal, neutral).
Learn words in phrases, noting collocations and grammatical features.
Some learners benefit from grouping vocabulary and using visual aids.
Reading and listening extensively are effective ways to learn new vocabulary.

examples.english: formal, informal or neutral
learn it in phrases
note pronunciation problems
reading and listening to as much English as possible

pitfalls: Do not treat vocabulary learning as memorizing definitions only.
Do not ignore grammar, pronunciation, or register when recording a new word.
Do not learn words only in isolation without phrases, groups, or context.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain two reasons why knowing a word means more than knowing its definition.
Describe two methods from this unit that could help you remember and expand new vocabulary more effectively.

chapters.title: Organising a vocabulary notebook
chapters.summary: This unit focuses on methods for organising a vocabulary notebook, going beyond simple lists to create a more effective learning tool. It covers grouping by meaning, building networks, recording collocations and synonyms/antonyms, and noting word class and stress.
points: Organize your notebook into sections based on broad categories like feelings, places, or movement.
Use charts and tables to visually structure vocabulary related to specific topics (e.g., music).
Create network diagrams to connect words and show relationships between them.
Record common collocations (words that typically go together) alongside new vocabulary.
Note synonyms (words with similar meanings) and antonyms (words with opposite meanings).
Always record the word class (noun, verb, adjective, etc.) and stress patterns of new words.

examples.english: classical (not classic)
win (prize, award, medal)
in a hurry
urban != rural
produce (verb) produce (noun) productive (adjective)

pitfalls: Do not record words only as isolated translations without grouping or context.
Do not forget to note collocations, word class, and stress patterns with new vocabulary.
Do not mix near-synonyms or antonyms without recording how they differ in use.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a way you could organize your vocabulary notebook to focus on words related to your hobbies.
Imagine a new vocabulary word you've learned. Explain how you would record it in your notebook using at least three of the methods described in this section.

chapters.title: Using your dictionary
chapters.summary: This unit explains how a good learner's dictionary provides more than definitions, including pronunciation guides, stress markings, usage examples, synonyms, antonyms, collocations, and information about word class and word relationships.
points: Dictionaries indicate pronunciation using symbols.
Dictionaries show word stress through markings or typography.
Dictionaries explain how words are used grammatically (usage).
Dictionaries provide synonyms (similar meaning) and antonyms (opposites).
Dictionaries list common collocations (words that go together).
Dictionaries differentiate between transitive and intransitive verbs.

examples.english: th in thick
sh in she
suggest + clause (not an infinitive) - I suggest you ring her right away.
mislay and misplace (synonyms)
friend != enemy/foe (antonyms)

pitfalls: Symbols used for pronunciation may differ from standard letters.
Incorrectly using a word without understanding its grammatical usage.
Failing to consider collocations when forming sentences.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain three kinds of information a good learner's dictionary can show beyond a basic definition.
Choose one word and describe what pronunciation, stress, grammar, and collocation information you would record from a dictionary entry.

chapters.title: Guessing and explaining meaning
chapters.summary: This unit focuses on strategies for understanding unfamiliar words by using context clues and relating them to known words or languages. It also provides useful phrases for explaining the meaning of unknown words to others.
points: Context provides clues to a word's meaning, including visual elements, background knowledge, surrounding words, and grammatical function.
Recognizing the structure of words, like prefixes and suffixes, can help decipher their meaning.
Similarities between English words and words in other languages can aid in understanding, but be wary of 'false friends'.
Certain phrases are useful for explaining the meaning of an unknown word.

examples.english: gladiolus
headscarf
gift in English means a present but in German Gift means poison
It's a kind of ...
It's got to be something you use for ...

pitfalls: Do not rely on one clue only when guessing the meaning of an unfamiliar word.
Do not confuse false friends with true equivalents in another language.
Do not ignore grammatical clues such as whether a word is a noun, adjective, or adverb.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain three clues you can use to guess the meaning of an unfamiliar word.
Choose one unfamiliar word and explain how you would guess its meaning from context, structure, or another language you know.

chapters.title: Countries, nationalities and languages
chapters.summary: This unit covers the conventions for referring to countries, nationalities, languages, and regional groups in English. It highlights rules for country names and the adjective patterns commonly used to form nationality words.
points: Some country names use 'the', including the United States, the United Kingdom, and the Netherlands.
Nationalities are often formed using suffixes like -ish, -(i)an, -ese, -i, and -ic.
Certain nationalities have unique noun forms, such as a Finn or a Spaniard.
Adjectives describing nationalities can often be used as nouns (e.g., a German).
Regional groups and ethnic groups, like 'Asian' or 'European,' can function as both adjectives and nouns.
People can be monolingual (one language), bilingual (two languages), or multilingual (multiple languages).

examples.english: the United States / the US(A)
British
a Spaniard
monolingual, bilingual, multilingual
Many Europeans enjoy travelling to the Far East.

pitfalls: Don't forget to use 'the' with countries like 'the United Kingdom'.
Not all nationalities follow the standard suffix rules; some (like 'Swiss') are exceptions.
Be mindful of whether a nationality is best expressed as an adjective or a noun (e.g., 'a German' vs 'a Dane').
When referring to people from a nationality, consider adding 'man,' 'woman,' or 'person' to avoid ambiguity.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain two rules from this unit about country names, nationalities, or language labels in English.
Write four short examples using nationality or region words from this unit, including at least one example with 'the'.

chapters.title: The weather
chapters.summary: This unit presents vocabulary for different types of weather, from cold conditions like frost and snow to warm, humid climates, as well as wet weather and wind.
points: Vocabulary is presented to describe cold, warm, wet, and windy weather.
A scale of wet weather intensity is provided, from 'shower' to 'flood'.
Different terms describe strong winds, including 'breeze', 'gale', and 'hurricane'.
The noun 'weather' is uncountable, meaning it is not used with a plural form.
Conditions like 'drought' describe periods of unusually low rainfall.

examples.english: mild, misty, foggy and damp
frost, icy roads, severe weather
stifling, muggy and humid
shower -> heavy rain -> downpour -> torrential rain -> flood
gentle breeze, gale, hurricane

pitfalls: Remember that 'weather' is an uncountable noun; you cannot say 'weathers'.
Avoid using 'severe weathers' - the correct phrase is 'severe weather'.
Confusing 'misty', 'foggy', and 'damp'-each describes a slightly different level of moisture and visibility.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain three differences between the vocabulary used for cold, hot, wet, and windy weather.
Write four short weather descriptions using vocabulary from this unit, including at least one term for rain and one term for wind.

chapters.title: Describing people: appearance
chapters.summary: This unit covers vocabulary for describing a person's appearance, including hair, face, build, and overall impression. It also provides alternatives to potentially impolite language and uses suffixes to describe approximate age or characteristics.
points: Describing hair includes length, texture (straight, wavy, curly), and color (blonde, fair, dark, ginger/red).
Facial features are described using adjectives like 'thin-faced,' 'round-faced,' and 'chubby face'.
Consider alternative vocabulary to avoid impoliteness when describing weight, such as 'plump,' 'stout,' 'stocky,' or 'overweight.'
Overall appearance can be described with adjectives like 'smart,' 'elegant,' 'scruffy,' 'stunning,' and 'good-looking'.
The suffix '-ish' can be used to describe approximate qualities, such as 'tallish' or 'brownish'.
'Fair' and 'dark' can describe hair, complexion or skin.

examples.english: She's got straight hair and she's thin-faced.
She's got a round face.
He's got receding hair and a few wrinkles.
She has such beautiful auburn hair.
He's very scruffy and untidy-looking.

pitfalls: Avoid using 'fat' directly; use alternatives like 'plump' or 'overweight'.
Be mindful of the formality of describing someone's appearance; some descriptions can be considered rude.
'Fair' and 'dark' can be confusing if the context isn't clear.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a person's appearance using at least three expressions from this unit for hair, face, build, or general appearance.
Explain two polite alternatives this unit gives for describing someone's build or general appearance.

chapters.title: Describing people: personality
chapters.summary: This unit explores vocabulary for describing people's personalities, covering intellectual ability, attitudes towards life, and interactions with others. It also highlights how the same characteristic can be viewed positively or negatively depending on perspective.
points: Several words describe intellectual ability, ranging from 'intelligent' to 'dim'.
People's attitudes towards life can be described as optimistic (looking on the bright side) or pessimistic (expecting the worst).
Extroverted individuals enjoy being the focus of attention, while introverted individuals prefer solitude.
Some qualities, like 'determined,' can have both positive (determined) and negative (obstinate) connotations.
Words describing attitude towards other people include 'sociable,' 'quarrelsome,' and 'honest.'
The phrase 'one person's meat is another person's poison' illustrates that characteristics can be perceived differently by different people.

examples.english: intelligent, bright, clever
pessimistic / optimistic
extroverted / introverted
sociable, quarrelsome, honest
determined / obstinate

pitfalls: Words like 'clever' can have negative connotations when used to describe someone tricking or deceiving others (cunning).
Characteristics considered positive in one context (e.g., being assertive) can be negative in another (e.g., being aggressive).
It's important to consider the context when judging whether a characteristic is positive or negative.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a person's personality using at least three contrasting terms from this unit.
Explain how one quality in this unit can sound positive in one context and negative in another.

chapters.title: Idioms describing people
chapters.summary: This unit explores English idioms used to describe people's personalities and behaviour, categorising them as positive, negative, or reflecting their relation to social norms. Many idioms use figurative language, such as body parts or unusual situations, to convey meaning.
points: Idioms provide colourful and informal ways to describe people's qualities.
Positive idioms often highlight kindness and helpfulness, while negative idioms express unpleasantness or difficulty.
Idioms based on 'head' often refer to mental qualities like memory, understanding, or composure.
Some idioms describe how someone relates to social norms, indicating they are eccentric or very conventional.
Certain idioms are commonly used to describe individuals in educational settings, such as a 'teacher's pet' or a 'know-all'.

examples.english: She has a heart of gold.
He's as hard as nails.
He's a nasty piece of work.
She's got her head screwed on.
to bury your head in the sand

pitfalls: Using idioms inappropriately can sound awkward or unnatural.
Some idioms are informal and may not be suitable for all situations.
Do not interpret idioms literally; their figurative meaning is what matters.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain the meaning of three idioms from this unit for describing people.
Write two short examples using idioms from this unit to describe someone's behaviour or personality.

chapters.title: Relationships
chapters.summary: This unit explores different types of relationships, from friendships and romantic connections to family ties, and how these relationships are formed and maintained, including online. It also introduces vocabulary for liking or disliking someone and common phrases used to describe relationships.
points: There's a spectrum of relationships, from casual acquaintances to best friends.
Social networks let people build contact lists, exchange messages, tag friends, and unfriend someone.
Vocabulary exists to express varying degrees of liking someone, ranging from 'like' to 'idolise', and disliking, from 'dislike' to 'loathe'.
Phrases and idioms provide a rich way to describe relationship dynamics and experiences.
Common phrases are used to describe starting and ending relationships.
People make friends, and it can sometimes be challenging to form new friendships, especially when relocating.

examples.english: She's a good friend - she's not my best friend.
I met her through my ex, Kallum.
I really fancy Charlotte, but her friend just leaves me cold.
Lily and I get on well.
I've fallen out with my parents again.

pitfalls: Confusing 'acquaintance' with 'best friend'.
Misunderstanding the severity of phrases like 'having an affair'.
Using incorrect vocabulary when expressing the intensity of feelings (e.g., using 'like' when 'idolise' is more appropriate).

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain three expressions from this unit for friendship, attraction, or disagreement.
Write four short examples using relationship vocabulary from this unit, including one expression for getting on well or badly with someone.

chapters.title: At home
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for types of homes, places in the home, and common everyday household objects.
points: A bungalow, semi-detached house, studio flat, cottage, and villa are all different housing types.
Places in the home include spaces such as the attic or loft, cellar, basement, porch, and landing.
Some rooms have specific functions, such as a utility room or a studio.
Outdoor home areas include a terrace or patio and a drive.
Everyday household vocabulary also covers small objects such as a charger, hanger, corkscrew, or peeler.

examples.english: bungalow, semi-detached house, studio flat
attic / loft, cellar, basement
porch, landing, terrace / patio, drive
remote control, phone charger, corkscrew

pitfalls: Do not confuse a cellar with a basement; a basement has windows while a cellar usually does not.
Do not assume a loft is only storage space; it can also be converted into living space.
Do not mix up places in the home with small household objects when learning the vocabulary.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain three vocabulary groups from this unit for types of homes, places in the home, or household objects.
Write four short examples using vocabulary from this unit for rooms, outdoor areas, or household objects.

chapters.title: Everyday minor problems
chapters.summary: This unit covers common everyday minor problems in the home, minor injuries, and small mishaps involving personal belongings.
points: Minor problems include things breaking, leaking, getting blocked, or stopping working.
The unit also covers minor injuries such as bruises, grazes, and twisted ankles.
Some problems affect possessions, such as stains, dents, or mislaid items.
Several words in this unit can be used both as nouns and as verbs.

examples.english: leaking pipe, cracked pot, chipped cup
power cut, washing machine broke down, computer keeps crashing
bruise, graze, twisted ankle
mislaid, stain, dent, locked myself out

pitfalls: Do not confuse problems in the home with minor injuries; the vocabulary groups are different.
Do not forget that words like crack, bruise, dent, and stain can be nouns or verbs.
Do not confuse a flat battery with batteries that have run out or are dead.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain three types of minor problems from this unit, for example in the home, with injuries, or with belongings.
Write four short examples using vocabulary from this unit for a household problem, an injury, or a damaged object.

chapters.title: Global problems
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for global disasters and tragedies, including natural events, human-caused incidents, and words for the people affected by them.
points: The unit covers disasters such as earthquakes, floods, droughts, volcanoes, famines, epidemics, hurricanes, and war.
Natural disasters are events not caused by human beings.
It also introduces verbs commonly used with disasters, such as erupt, spread, starve, shake, break out, and sweep through.
People affected by disasters may be described as casualties, victims, survivors, or refugees.

examples.english: earthquake, flood, drought, famine
epidemic, explosion, hurricane, civil war
casualties, victims, survivors, refugees
erupted, spread, starving, broke out

pitfalls: Confusing 'casualty' with 'victim' - casualties specifically refer to the dead and injured.
Assuming all major accidents are natural disasters - the text distinguishes between the two.
Not recognizing that 'refugees' need shelter elsewhere due to the disaster.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain three vocabulary groups from this unit for disasters, disaster verbs, or people affected by disasters.
Write four short examples using vocabulary from this unit for a disaster, its effects, or the people involved.

chapters.title: Education
chapters.summary: This unit outlines the stages of education in the UK system and introduces vocabulary for schools, exams, grades, and educational technology.
points: The UK system includes stages such as creche, nursery school, primary school, secondary school, and sixth-form college.
Secondary education may take place in comprehensive schools, grammar schools, or private fee-paying schools.
Exam language includes take, do, sit, or resit an exam, as well as marks, grades, pass, and fail.
Education technology includes LMS and VLE systems that support distance or blended learning.
Common questions include the school-leaving age and the age at which compulsory education begins.

examples.english: creche, nursery school, primary school, secondary school
comprehensive school, grammar school, public school
take / do / sit / resit an exam
marks, grades, high grade, minimum grade
LMS, VLE, distance learning, blended learning

pitfalls: Do not confuse UK public schools with state schools; public schools are private and fee-paying.
Do not mix up comprehensive schools and grammar schools.
Do not confuse passing an exam with simply taking or sitting it.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain three stages or institutions in the UK education system from this unit.
Write four short examples using vocabulary from this unit for schools, exams, grades, or online learning.

chapters.title: Higher education
chapters.summary: This unit explains aspects of higher education in the UK, including undergraduate and postgraduate study, university teaching formats, and student services.
points: Undergraduate studies lead to a bachelor's degree and involve modules, credits, and often a dissertation.
Postgraduate studies include a master's degree and a PhD, and require specialisation and research.
University teaching formats include lectures, seminars, and tutorials.
Student services include admissions, funding, tuition fees, student loans, scholarships, and the student union.

examples.english: undergraduate studies, bachelor's degree, modules, credits
postgraduate studies, master's degree, PhD, thesis
lecture theatre, seminar, tutorial, halls of residence
admissions, tuition fees, student loan, scholarship

pitfalls: Do not confuse undergraduate and postgraduate study stages.
Do not mix up a dissertation with a thesis.
Do not confuse tuition fees, student loans, and scholarships.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain three vocabulary groups from this unit for study stages, teaching formats, or student services.
Write four short examples using vocabulary from this unit for university study, classes, or funding.

chapters.title: Work
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for jobs, work patterns, and employment conditions. It also shows common collocations with job and work and useful expressions for talking about working life.
points: Job titles include executive, director, manager, supervisor, union representative, and unskilled worker.
{'Job and work are used differently': 'you get, apply for, or take a job, but you do work or work in a place.'}
Employment conditions and changes include maternity leave, shifts, promotion, dismissal, and retirement.
Work patterns include part-time work, full-time work, shift work, and nine-to-five work.
Some occupations are described as a trade or a profession.
Useful expressions include earn a living and delegate work.

examples.english: executive, supervisor, union representative
get a job / apply for a job / take a job
do some plumbing work / work in an office
on maternity leave / work shifts / work nine-to-five
earn a living / delegate work

pitfalls: Do not use job and work as if they were always interchangeable.
Do not record only the occupation word and ignore the collocations that go with it.
Do not forget that expressions for work conditions often differ across countries and workplaces.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between job and work, using at least two collocations from this unit.
Describe a working situation using vocabulary such as shifts, maternity leave, promotion, or nine-to-five.

chapters.title: Business
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for starting, running, and expanding a business. It focuses on business plans, markets, customers, competition, and common commercial collocations.
points: A business plan sets out aims, strategy, and priorities for a company.
Businesses can be launched, rolled out, expanded, or run by a corporation or firm.
Companies do business, enter new markets, access customers, and build contacts.
Market research helps identify potential customers and understand demand.
Business language often includes profit, risk, recession, competition, and order books.
Customer care and regular feedback are important parts of business operations.

examples.english: put forward a business plan / launch a business
roll out an expanded range / manufacture machines
enter new markets / build contacts
market research / potential customers / customer care
make a profit / face stiff competition / take a risk

pitfalls: Do not treat business plan as a general idea; it refers to a concrete plan for the business.
Do not confuse customers with markets; one is the people, the other is the commercial area.
Do not learn profit, risk, and competition in isolation without their common verb combinations.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe how a company could launch a new product, using vocabulary such as business plan, market research, and potential customers.
Explain how a business might respond to recession or stiff competition, using at least two collocations from this unit.

chapters.title: Sport
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for sports, equipment, athletics, and competition. It also covers common terms for players, spectators, and sporting results.
points: Sports vocabulary includes activities such as hang-gliding, snowboarding, scuba diving, and archery.
Different sports use different equipment, such as a club, racket, cue, paddle, or bow.
Athletics includes events like the pole vault, discus, javelin, high jump, and marathon.
Competition language includes set a record, hold a record, qualify for, and get knocked out.
People in sport can be described as footballers, swimmers, gymnasts, jockeys, or archers.
Spectators and fans are related ideas, but they are not always exactly the same.

examples.english: hang-gliding / snowboarding / scuba diving
club / racket / cue / bow
pole vault / javelin / marathon
set a record / hold the record / qualify for the semi-final
spectator / fan / trophy

pitfalls: Do not mix up sports that use similar equipment, such as billiards, pool, and snooker.
Do not confuse set a record with hold the record.
Do not forget the person word when learning a sport word, for example archer from archery.

practicePrompts.prompt: Compare two sports from this unit and include vocabulary for equipment, people, or competition.
Describe a competition using expressions such as qualify for, get knocked out, trophy, or hold the record.

chapters.title: Art and literature
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for literature, the fine arts, and the performing arts. It highlights useful distinctions between artists, art forms, and literary genres.
points: Literature includes poetry, novels, short stories, biographies, and other written forms.
The fine arts include painting, sculpture, architecture, and ceramics.
The performing arts include dance, ballet, drama, opera, cinema, and concerts.
A sculptor creates sculpture, a potter produces pottery, and an architect works in architecture.
Common art categories include portrait, landscape, still life, abstract painting, and original painting.
Literary discussion often uses words such as plot, characters, historical setting, critics, and bestseller.

examples.english: poetry / novels / short stories / biographies
painting / sculpture / architecture / ceramics
ballet / drama / opera / concerts
portrait / landscape / still life / abstract painting
plot / characters / historical setting / bestseller

pitfalls: Do not confuse the artist with the work, for example sculptor with sculpture or statue.
Do not use art and arts as if they always meant the same thing.
Do not learn literature words without the genre labels that help classify them.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between literature, the fine arts, and the performing arts, using examples from this unit.
Describe a book, painting, or performance using vocabulary such as plot, characters, portrait, landscape, or abstract painting.

chapters.title: Theatre and cinema
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for theatre and cinema, including productions, performances, reviews, and the language used to describe films and stage events.
points: Theatre vocabulary includes production, sets, costumes, cast, direction, and performance.
Review language includes rave reviews, comedian, entertainer, audience, and viewer.
Film vocabulary includes opening scene, script, dialogue, screenplay, score, and director.
A film or play can be set in a particular place or period and can capture a mood or atmosphere.
Stage, venue, show, and appearance are useful words for talking about arts events more generally.
Words like cinema, theatre, opera, and ballet can be used both generally and for specific events.

examples.english: production / sets / costumes / cast / direction
rave reviews / audience / viewer / comedian / entertainer
opening scene / script / dialogue / screenplay / score
set in Paris / capture the atmosphere
stage / venue / show / appearance

pitfalls: Do not confuse cast with costumes or sets; they refer to different parts of a production.
Do not use audience and viewer as if they always referred to the same kind of spectator.
Do not learn theatre and cinema words without the common combinations used in reviews and descriptions.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a film or theatre production using vocabulary such as cast, dialogue, score, sets, or rave reviews.
Explain the difference between general words such as theatre or cinema and the more specific production vocabulary from this unit.

chapters.title: Music
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for music technology, musical tastes, band roles, and common musical collocations. It also highlights a few common usage mistakes.
points: Music files can be downloaded as albums or individual tracks and organised into playlists.
Files can be synced between devices or streamed from one device to another.
Band vocabulary includes lead singer, lead guitar, bass guitarist, keyboard player, and drummer.
Music types include classical, folk, jazz, R&B, hip-hop, rap, film soundtracks, and canned music.
Useful collocations include read music, play by ear, have a good ear, and pick out a tune.
Music is uncountable, and the correct expression is classical music, not classic music.

examples.english: album / track / playlist / sync / stream
lead singer / lead guitar / bass guitarist / drummer
classical music / folk music / film soundtrack / canned music
read music / play by ear / have a good ear / pick out a tune

pitfalls: Do not use music as a countable noun.
Do not say classic music when you mean classical music.
Do not forget the difference between an album and a track.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe your musical tastes using vocabulary such as classical, folk, hip-hop, soundtrack, or canned music.
Explain how someone could organise and listen to music using words such as album, track, playlist, sync, and stream.

chapters.title: Food
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for taste, food quality, eating out, and hosting meals at home. It combines descriptive adjectives with common restaurant and dinner-table expressions.
points: Taste adjectives include hot, spicy, salty, sugary, sour, bitter, tasty, bland, and moreish.
Food quality can be described as overcooked, undercooked, ripe, unripe, gone off, light, or stodgy.
Health-related food terms include organic food, junk food, processed food, and a balanced diet.
Restaurant vocabulary includes make a reservation, cancel a booking, starter, main course, dessert, side dish, and set menu.
Hosting language includes seconds, help yourself, pudding, re-fill your glass, and Say when.
Some nouns and expressions are fixed, such as savoury snacks and a-la-carte dishes.

examples.english: spicy / mild / bland / tasteless / moreish
overcooked / undercooked / ripe / gone off / stodgy
organic food / junk food / processed food / balanced diet
make a reservation / main course / side dish / set menu
seconds / help yourself / pudding / re-fill your glass

pitfalls: Do not confuse bitter with sour.
Do not use bland as a positive description.
Do not forget that moreish means you want to eat more of something.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a meal using vocabulary such as spicy, bland, ripe, overcooked, or moreish.
Explain what happens when you eat out, using words such as reservation, starter, main course, side dish, and dessert.

chapters.title: Physical geography
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for rivers, mountains, coasts, and other geographical features. It also explains important patterns for using the definite article with place names.
points: Physical geography vocabulary includes coast, valley, glacier, peak, source, ridge, and delta.
Sea and river terms include current, estuary, shore, strait, cape, and peninsula.
Individual mountains usually do not take the, but mountain chains do.
Rivers, oceans, seas, gulfs, and many groups of islands normally take the.
Country names usually do not take the, except in names like the USA or when followed by a limiting phrase.
Limiting phrases can change the article pattern, as in the Japan of today.

examples.english: current / estuary / source / shore / strait / peninsula
peak / ridge / valley / glacier / mountain chain
the Himalayas / the Gulf of Mexico / the USA
the Japan of today / the Brazil of the future

pitfalls: Do not add the automatically before every country or mountain.
Do not forget that rivers and seas usually take the.
Do not ignore limiting phrases that change the normal article pattern.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a landscape using vocabulary such as valley, ridge, glacier, coast, or estuary.
Explain when the is used with geographical names, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Environmental problems
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for major environmental problems, their causes, and possible responses. It also includes useful compound nouns and policy language.
points: Key problems include global warming, smog, acid rain, water pollution, hazardous waste, and destruction of rainforests.
The greenhouse effect is linked to carbon dioxide and rising global temperatures.
Plant and animal life may become endangered or extinct because of environmental damage.
The prefix over- appears in words such as overfishing and overpopulated.
Solutions and responses include nature reserves, recycling, organic food, and reducing fossil fuel use.
Environmental language often includes carbon footprint, climate change, ozone layer, and polar ice caps.

examples.english: global warming / climate change / carbon dioxide / greenhouse effect
hazardous waste / water pollution / smog / acid rain
endangered species / extinct / destruction of rainforests
overfishing / overpopulated / carbon footprint / fossil fuels
nature reserve / organic food / recycle / environmentally friendly

pitfalls: Do not confuse extinct with endangered.
Do not treat global warming and climate change as identical in every context.
Do not learn carbon footprint or fossil fuels without their environmental context.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe an environmental problem using vocabulary such as smog, hazardous waste, endangered species, or carbon footprint.
Explain two ways people or governments could respond to environmental problems, using collocations from this unit.

chapters.title: Towns
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for describing towns and cities, their facilities, their character, and their problems. It combines descriptive language with common urban collocations.
points: Town and city descriptions often focus on the centre, residential districts, facilities, and local attractions.
Useful facilities vocabulary includes sports centre, art gallery, museum, science park, and market.
Urban problems include traffic, overcrowding, pollution, vandalism, and slums.
Adjectives such as picturesque, bustling, historic, run-down, and fashionable help describe a place.
A place may be well worth visiting or within walking distance of the centre.
Location language includes overlook the harbour, on the site of, and on the outskirts.

examples.english: city centre / residential district / science park / market / museum
picturesque / bustling / run-down / fashionable / historic
within walking distance / well worth visiting / overlook the harbour
traffic / overcrowding / pollution / vandalism / slums

pitfalls: Do not use city and town as exact synonyms in every context.
Do not forget that many town descriptions rely on collocations, not just single adjectives.
Do not confuse a facility with a problem, for example gallery with vandalism or market with traffic.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a town or city using vocabulary such as city centre, residential district, picturesque, or bustling.
Explain the main attractions and problems of a town, using at least two collocations from this unit.

chapters.title: The natural world
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for animals, plants, and natural processes. It also highlights useful verb patterns such as lay eggs, blossom, breed, pick, and pick up.
points: Animal and plant vocabulary includes mane, beak, hoof, whiskers, petal, stem, root, and thorn.
Natural processes include blossom, grow, breed, lay eggs, and flower.
Some words describe movement or physical features, such as paws, claws, bark, and branch.
The unit distinguishes pick from pick up.
Pick is used for gathering flowers or fruit, while pick up is used for lifting something that has dropped.
Nature vocabulary often groups into animal words and plant words.

examples.english: whiskers / paws / claws / hoof / mane
petal / stem / root / bud / thorn
blossom / grow / lay eggs / breed
pick flowers / pick up a purse / drop from a tree

pitfalls: Do not use pick up when you mean pick flowers or fruit from a plant.
Do not confuse plant parts with animal parts.
Do not ignore the verb pattern when learning nature vocabulary.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe part of the natural world using vocabulary such as whiskers, petal, root, thorn, or branch.
Explain the difference between pick and pick up, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Clothes
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for clothes, alterations, fit, and personal style. It also reviews some important collocations and plural clothing nouns.
points: Clothing vocabulary includes items such as belt, sole, laces, hoodie, slippers, helmet, and swimming costume.
Some clothing words are usually plural, such as trousers, shorts, tights, jeans, and underpants.
Alteration verbs include let out, let down, take in, and take up.
Adjectives for clothes include baggy, close-fitting, checked, woollen, suede, and denim.
Style language includes dressed casually, elegant, designer sunglasses, and inside out.
Fit, match, and suit have different meanings when describing clothes.

examples.english: belt / sole / laces / hoodie / slippers / helmet
jeans / shorts / tights / underpants / a pair of trousers
let out / let down / take in / take up
baggy / close-fitting / checked / denim / woollen
fit / match / suit / dressed casually / inside out

pitfalls: Do not forget that many clothes for the lower body are plural.
Do not confuse fit, match, and suit.
Do not use alteration verbs as if they all meant the same change.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe what someone is wearing, using vocabulary such as checked, close-fitting, denim, hoodie, or belt.
Explain how a piece of clothing could be altered, using verbs such as let out, let down, take in, or take up.

chapters.title: Health and medicine
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for symptoms, illnesses, treatment, and recovery. It includes both everyday expressions and more formal medical language.
points: Common symptoms include rash, bruise, lump, spots, black eye, indigestion, and sunburn.
Sensation verbs include ache, itch, shiver, tremble, and hurt.
Informal illness expressions include pick up a bug and get over a cold or infection.
Treatment vocabulary includes bandage, injection, plaster, teaspoonful, tablet, and operation.
Serious health language includes suffer a stroke, contract AIDS, and die of a heart attack.
Health vocabulary often combines verbs, nouns, and prepositions in fixed patterns.

examples.english: rash / bruise / indigestion / black eye / sore throat
ache / itch / shiver / tremble / feel dizzy
pick up a bug / get over an infection
bandage / injection / plaster / tablet / operation
suffer a stroke / die of a heart attack

pitfalls: Do not say die with a heart attack when you mean die of a heart attack.
Do not forget the preposition in operate on someone.
Do not mix up everyday symptom language with formal medical diagnosis language.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a health problem using vocabulary such as rash, bruise, indigestion, shiver, or injection.
Explain how someone could recover from an illness, using at least two expressions from this unit.

chapters.title: Medicine and technology
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for the history of medical technology and possible future developments. It covers inventions, devices, surgery, and remote diagnosis.
points: Early medical technology included spectacles, crutches, wheelchairs, and X-rays.
Modern devices include hearing aids, pacemakers, contact lenses, and prosthetic limbs.
Medical technology can identify, diagnose, treat, and monitor disease.
Future developments include remote diagnosis, databases, transplants, and robotic limbs.
Keyhole surgery reduces the need to cut open the body during operations.
Medical technology vocabulary often combines technology terms with treatment verbs.

examples.english: spectacles / crutches / wheelchair / X-rays
hearing aid / pacemaker / contact lenses / artificial hip
identify disease / diagnose a patient / treat an illness
keyhole surgery / database / transplant / robotic limb

pitfalls: Do not treat diagnose, identify, and treat as synonyms.
Do not forget that sophisticated describes complexity, not just cost.
Do not learn a device word without knowing what medical problem it helps with.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe two examples of medical technology and explain what they help doctors or patients do.
Explain how future medicine could change through technology, using vocabulary such as keyhole surgery, diagnose, database, or transplant.

chapters.title: Health and lifestyle
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for diet, healthy and unhealthy food, fitness, stress, and long-term health effects. It also includes useful verb-noun collocations connected with health.
points: Some foods are beneficial, while others may cause long-term health problems if consumed in large quantities.
Useful food collocations include wholemeal bread, fizzy drinks, oily fish, processed food, and sugary foods.
Some foods may boost the immune system, lower cholesterol, reduce the risk of heart disease, or slow the ageing process.
Health vocabulary includes mental health, fitness, exercise, stress, and depression.
Lifestyle language often links diet, exercise, and stressful work to physical and mental wellbeing.
Food and fruit can be countable or uncountable depending on the meaning, while fish is often treated differently in food contexts.

examples.english: wholemeal bread / fizzy drinks / oily fish / processed food
boost the immune system / lower cholesterol / reduce the risk of heart disease
slow the ageing process / improve short-term memory
mental health / stressful job / regular exercise / fitness

pitfalls: Do not confuse countable and uncountable uses of food and fruit.
Do not learn the health verbs without the nouns they typically collocate with.
Do not treat healthy lifestyle vocabulary as only about food; it also includes exercise and stress.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a healthy lifestyle using vocabulary such as wholemeal bread, oily fish, regular exercise, stress, or mental health.
Explain how food or daily habits can affect health, using at least two collocations from this unit.

chapters.title: Travel
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for transport, journeys, and common travel situations. It also clarifies important differences between words such as trip, journey, travel, and voyage.
points: Travel vocabulary is often grouped by road, rail, sea, and air.
Vehicle words include coach, van, lorry, liner, ferry, aircraft, and helicopter.
{'Travel words are not interchangeable': 'trip is often shorter, journey is more general, travel is uncountable, and voyage is usually long and often by sea.'}
Transport collocations include delayed flight, put someone up in a hotel, on standby, turbulence, and bumpy landing.
Sea travel expressions include rough sea, calm sea, crew member, and get seasick.
Common mistakes include saying a travel and using in time when on time is needed.

examples.english: coach / lorry / high-speed train / ferry / aircraft
platform / runway / cabin / cockpit / deck
trip / journey / travel / voyage
delayed flight / on standby / turbulence / bumpy landing
put me up in a hotel / on time / get seasick

pitfalls: Do not say a travel because travel is uncountable.
Do not confuse trip and journey in fixed expressions such as business trip.
Do not use in time when you mean punctually; use on time.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a journey using vocabulary such as trip, journey, delayed flight, turbulence, or on time.
Explain the difference between trip, journey, travel, and voyage, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Holidays
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for accommodation, holiday activities, and the descriptive language used in holiday brochures and messages. It also highlights typical promotional collocations.
points: Holiday accommodation includes campsite, caravan, youth hostel, self-catering apartment, and package holiday.
Holiday activities include sunbathing, sightseeing, going on excursions, and getting away from it all.
Brochure language often uses strong positive adjectives such as breathtaking, luxurious, exclusive, and fascinating.
Common holiday collocations include magnificent mountains, sandy beach, lively resort, and guided tour.
Holiday advertisements often exaggerate, so brochure language should be learned as promotional vocabulary.
Travel and holiday descriptions often depend on adjective-noun combinations rather than single words.

examples.english: campsite / caravan / youth hostel / self-catering apartment
package holiday / excursion / guided tour / resort
breathtaking scenery / luxurious hotel / exclusive beach
magnificent mountains / sandy beach / fascinating town

pitfalls: Do not assume brochure adjectives are neutral; they are often promotional.
Do not learn accommodation words without the collocations that describe them.
Do not confuse self-catering with full-board or hotel-style accommodation.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a holiday destination using vocabulary such as campsite, resort, breathtaking, luxurious, or excursion.
Explain what kind of holiday you would choose, using at least two accommodation words and two brochure-style adjectives from this unit.

chapters.title: Science and technology
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for modern science and everyday technology. It covers scientific fields, technical verbs, and the language used to describe devices and innovations.
points: Science vocabulary includes genetics, molecular biology, ergonomics, hydroponics, and stem cell research.
Technology vocabulary includes smartphone, 3D TV, printer, button, feature, and device.
Scientific verbs include modify, engineer, extract, experiment, and utilise.
Some terms describe applied science, such as voice technology and genetic engineering.
Technical language often combines nouns and verbs in fixed patterns, such as press a button or turn on a printer.
Technology is broader than computers and includes scientific and practical inventions.

examples.english: genetic engineering / GM foods / stem cell research
ergonomics / hydroponics / molecular biology
device / feature / printer / smartphone / 3D TV
experiment with / utilise / extract / turn on / press a button

pitfalls: Do not assume technology only refers to digital devices.
Do not confuse scientific fields with the products or inventions that come from them.
Do not use formal technical verbs such as utilise without understanding their register.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a modern scientific or technological development using vocabulary such as genetic engineering, device, feature, or experiment.
Explain the difference between a scientific field and a piece of technology, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Computers
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for computer hardware, software, and common user actions. It also includes useful collocations connected with files, data, and upgrades.
points: Hardware refers to the physical parts of a computer, such as the hard disk, screen, and microchip.
Software refers to programs, applications, and operating tools such as word processors and spreadsheets.
Common computer words include laptop, desktop computer, PC, app, network, and file.
Useful actions include back up data, undo a change, upgrade a system, and download a file.
Many computer tasks are described through fixed verb-noun collocations rather than single words.
Good computer vocabulary often pairs a device word with the action normally used with it.

examples.english: hardware / software / app / network / file
laptop / desktop computer / PC / hard disk / microchip
back up data / undo a change / upgrade a system
word processor / spreadsheet / application

pitfalls: Do not confuse hardware with software.
Do not treat back up as the same as save.
Do not learn a device name without the action it is commonly used with.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe how you use a computer, using vocabulary such as laptop, app, network, file, or word processor.
Explain the difference between hardware and software, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Communications and the Internet
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for the Internet, websites, and online communication. It focuses on the difference between key terms and on common actions people perform online.
points: The Internet is the worldwide network, while the Web is one part of it.
Internet vocabulary includes wi-fi, browser, search engine, website, home page, and bookmark.
Communication terms include email, attachment, FAQ, log in, and log out.
Search language includes search engine, Google, and googling.
Online actions are usually described through fixed phrases such as browse the Web, log in, or bookmark a page.
Internet vocabulary often mixes technical nouns with everyday verbs.

examples.english: Internet / Web / wi-fi / browser / search engine
website / home page / bookmark / FAQ / attachment
log in / log out / surf the net / google something
website / institution / page / connection

pitfalls: Do not use Internet and Web as if they were identical in every context.
Do not forget the verb patterns with log in and log out.
Do not learn internet terms without the actions that normally go with them.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain how someone can find information online, using vocabulary such as browser, search engine, website, bookmark, or FAQ.
Describe the difference between the Internet and the Web, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Social media
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for social media activity, online privacy, and common app features. It also highlights how many social media words function as both nouns and verbs.
points: Social media words often work as both nouns and verbs, such as post, comment, and share.
Common actions include follow, subscribe, like, rate, and send a direct message.
Social media users need to understand privacy settings and account management.
Notifications, posts, comments, and shares describe common app activity.
Social media vocabulary often mixes platform features with personal communication language.
Online safety language is an important part of this topic.

examples.english: post / comment / share / follow / subscribe
like a post / rate a video / direct message / notification
privacy settings / account / delete an old account
app / adventure / profile / personal information

pitfalls: Do not share personal information without checking privacy settings.
Do not forget that many social media words can be both nouns and verbs.
Do not confuse a public post with a direct message.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe what people can do on social media using vocabulary such as post, comment, share, follow, or direct message.
Explain why privacy settings matter on social media, using at least two expressions from this unit.

chapters.title: The press and the media
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for television, newspapers, and the wider mass media. It covers programme types, newspaper styles, and common media collocations.
points: Media vocabulary includes documentary, sitcom, game show, presenter, subtitles, and dubbing.
The mass media includes television, radio, newspapers, and the Internet.
Newspaper language contrasts tabloids with quality newspapers.
Broadcast vocabulary includes broadcast, channel, series, and set in.
News is uncountable and normally takes a singular verb.
Media descriptions often depend on programme-type labels and fixed expressions.

examples.english: documentary / sitcom / game show / presenter
subtitles / dubbing / channel / broadcast / series
tabloid / quality newspaper / celebrity news / crime story
the news is on / set in London / shot in black and white

pitfalls: Do not use a plural verb with news.
Do not confuse tabloids with quality newspapers.
Do not learn programme words without the type labels that classify them.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a TV or newspaper item using vocabulary such as documentary, sitcom, tabloid, subtitles, or presenter.
Explain the difference between tabloid and quality newspaper coverage, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Politics and public institutions
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for political systems, elections, and public institutions. It contrasts presidential and parliamentary systems and includes key words for representation and government.
points: Political systems include republic, monarchy, dictatorship, presidential government, and parliamentary government.
Election vocabulary includes candidate, constituency, electorate, majority, seat, and term.
The Prime Minister and President have different roles depending on the political system.
Public institutions and political language often focus on representation and power.
Government vocabulary often combines abstract nouns with election and leadership verbs.
Political terms need context because they are system-specific.

examples.english: republic / monarchy / dictatorship / parliamentary government / presidential government
Prime Minister / President / Cabinet / Parliament
candidate / constituency / electorate / majority / seat / term
general election / represent voters / appoint officials

pitfalls: Do not assume presidential and parliamentary systems work in the same way.
Do not confuse electorate with constituency.
Do not treat monarchy as identical to absolute rule in every context.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between a presidential and a parliamentary system, using examples from this unit.
Describe an election using vocabulary such as candidate, constituency, majority, seat, or electorate.

chapters.title: Crime
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for crimes, criminal investigations, and court procedures. It also covers the people involved in trials and the language of punishment.
points: Crime vocabulary includes shoplifting, burglary, terrorism, robbery, and accomplice.
Investigation and court language includes evidence, jury, verdict, sentence, and plead not guilty.
Crime nouns often have related verbs, such as burgle, shoplift, rob, and terrorise.
Legal outcomes include guilty, not guilty, prison, sentence, and early release.
Crime vocabulary often depends on role words such as criminal, judge, jury, witness, and accomplice.
Legal terms are closely tied to courtroom stages and decisions.

examples.english: criminal / crime / accomplice / evidence / witness
shoplifting / burglary / robbery / terrorism
plead not guilty / jury / verdict / sentence
prison / early release / good behaviour

pitfalls: Do not confuse the crime noun with its related verb.
Do not mix up verdict and sentence.
Do not learn court vocabulary without the people who use or decide it.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a crime case using vocabulary such as evidence, jury, verdict, sentence, or accomplice.
Explain the difference between a crime, a criminal, and the legal process after an arrest, using expressions from this unit.

chapters.title: Money
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for personal finance, bank services, and public finance. It covers accounts, borrowing, debt, and the main taxes people encounter.
points: Personal finance vocabulary includes current account, savings account, overdraft, credit limit, loan, and mortgage.
Bank service language includes direct debit, transfer money, online banking, and cash machine.
Financial condition words include income, outgoings, expenses, in credit, overdrawn, and in the red.
Public finance includes income tax, inheritance tax, customs duty, VAT, and corporation tax.
Money vocabulary often depends on collocations such as raise money, pay interest, or improve your credit rating.
Finance language mixes everyday banking with more formal tax terminology.

examples.english: current account / savings account / overdraft / mortgage
direct debit / transfer money / online banking / cash machine
income / outgoings / expenses / in credit / in the red
income tax / inheritance tax / customs duty / VAT

pitfalls: Do not confuse in credit with overdrawn.
Do not forget that overdrafts and loans involve interest and limits.
Do not mix up personal finance vocabulary with public tax terms.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe how someone manages money using vocabulary such as current account, direct debit, overdraft, mortgage, or savings account.
Explain the difference between personal finance and public finance, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Describing objects
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for describing objects, materials, and physical qualities. It also includes adjective-noun pairs and common spoken intensifiers.
points: Opposites include artificial and natural, vivid and sombre, transparent and opaque, stiff and flexible.
Object-description vocabulary also includes delicate, tough, fragile, sturdy, genuine, and fake.
Some adjectives have related nouns such as decency, precision, severity, solidity, and triviality.
These words can describe both physical objects and more abstract qualities.
Spoken English often uses modifiers such as great big, reasonably good, pretty thick, and nowhere near as.
Descriptions often depend on contrast pairs rather than isolated words.

examples.english: artificial / natural / vivid / sombre / transparent / opaque
delicate / tough / fragile / sturdy / stiff / flexible
genuine / fake / solid oak / precise measurements
great big / reasonably good / pretty thick / nowhere near as

pitfalls: Do not confuse fragile with flexible or delicate with weak.
Do not use fake when you mean simply poor quality; it means not genuine.
Do not ignore the spoken modifiers that often go with descriptive adjectives.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe an object using vocabulary such as fragile, opaque, sturdy, vivid, or fake.
Explain the difference between two opposite adjectives from this unit and give an example for each.

chapters.title: Belief and opinion
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for expressing beliefs, opinions, and degrees of certainty. It also highlights common opinion phrases and some important preposition patterns.
points: Belief verbs include think, believe, maintain, reckon, suspect, doubt, and be convinced.
Opinion phrases include in my view, to my mind, if you ask me, and from my point of view.
Different words express different levels of certainty, doubt, or personal judgment.
Point of view can refer to perspective as well as opinion.
Belief vocabulary also includes adjectives such as fanatical, moderate, and firm.
Prepositions are important in this topic, especially in fixed phrases such as to my mind.

examples.english: believe / suspect / doubt / be convinced / maintain
in my view / to my mind / if you ask me / from my point of view
fanatical / moderate / firm beliefs
convinced that / doubt whether / maintain that

pitfalls: Do not say in my mind when you mean to my mind.
Do not confuse suspect with believe because suspect often suggests something negative or uncertain.
Do not treat all opinion phrases as equally formal.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain how you would express a strong opinion and a doubtful opinion, using vocabulary from this unit.
Describe a topic from two different points of view, using at least two opinion phrases from this unit.

chapters.title: Pleasant and unpleasant feelings
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for pleasant and unpleasant feelings and shows how strength words such as absolutely and quite affect meaning.
points: Pleasant feelings include content, grateful, delighted, thrilled, and relieved.
Unpleasant feelings include miserable, anxious, furious, fed up, and sick and tired.
Some adjectives describe strong emotions and naturally combine with absolutely.
Weaker feeling adjectives often combine with quite in the sense of rather.
Feeling vocabulary can describe both short reactions and longer emotional states.
Register and intensity matter when choosing between near-synonyms.

examples.english: content / grateful / delighted / thrilled / relieved
miserable / anxious / furious / fed up / sick and tired
absolutely delighted / absolutely furious / quite anxious
contented person / grateful to someone / furious with someone

pitfalls: Do not use absolutely with every feeling adjective.
Do not confuse content with contented in patterns where only one form is natural.
Do not ignore prepositions such as grateful to and furious with.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a situation using vocabulary such as delighted, grateful, anxious, or furious.
Explain the difference between a mild feeling and a strong feeling, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Like, dislike and desire
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for liking, disliking, wanting, and anticipating. It covers differences in strength, formality, and emotional tone.
points: Strong dislike can be expressed with loathe, detest, despise, cannot stand, and cannot bear.
Negative reactions can be described with repel, revolt, appal, and disgust.
Wanting and anticipation vocabulary includes desire, long for, yearn for, look forward to, and dread.
Positive attraction can be expressed with fascinate, appeal to, and captivate.
Some words in this area are formal or literary, especially desire and yearn.
Different expressions take different grammar patterns, including noun phrases and -ing forms.

examples.english: loathe / detest / despise / cannot stand / cannot bear
repel / revolt / appal / disgust
desire / long for / yearn for / look forward to / dread
fascinate / appeal to / captivate

pitfalls: Do not use all dislike words as if they were the same strength.
Do not forget that some expressions, such as look forward to, are followed by -ing or a noun.
Do not use desire casually without noticing its formal or sometimes sexual meaning.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe something you strongly like or dislike using at least two expressions from this unit.
Explain the difference between looking forward to something and dreading it, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Speaking
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for reporting speech, describing tone, and adding attitude through reporting verbs and adverbs.
points: Speaking verbs can show loudness, softness, emotion, and intention.
Reporting verbs include whisper, mutter, mumble, murmur, shout, boast, insist, and threaten.
Adverbs such as angrily, calmly, or nervously add detail to the way someone speaks.
Different verbs suggest different speaker attitudes or emotional states.
Reporting clauses are often built with that or with direct speech.
These verbs help describe how something was said, not just what was said.

examples.english: whisper / mutter / mumble / murmur / shout
boast / insist / threaten / protest / complain
speak angrily / calmly / nervously / softly
threaten that / insist that / whisper that

pitfalls: Do not confuse similar reporting verbs such as mutter, mumble, and murmur.
Do not add adverbs randomly; they should match the tone and situation.
Do not ignore the difference between describing what was said and how it was said.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a conversation using vocabulary such as whisper, shout, insist, threaten, or angrily.
Explain the difference between two reporting verbs from this unit and give an example of each.

chapters.title: The six senses
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary connected with the five senses and the idea of a sixth sense. It also includes useful adjectives and verbs for describing sensory experiences.
points: The five senses are sight, hearing, taste, touch, and smell, and a sixth sense refers to extrasensory perception.
Sense verbs are often followed by adjectives, as in looks dreadful or sounds marvellous.
Taste and smell vocabulary includes sweet, salty, bitter, sour, spicy, fragrant, and pungent.
Touch vocabulary includes verbs such as poke, stroke, pat, grasp, grab, snatch, and press.
Sixth-sense vocabulary includes telepathy, intuition, premonition, and deja vu.
Sensory vocabulary often depends on contrasts between literal and figurative meaning.

examples.english: look dreadful / sound marvellous / smell delicious
sweet / salty / bitter / sour / spicy / pungent
poke / stroke / pat / grasp / grab / snatch / press
telepathy / intuition / premonition / deja vu

pitfalls: Do not confuse tasty with tasteful.
Do not forget that hot can refer to spice as well as temperature.
Do not use adverbs after sense verbs when an adjective is needed.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe an experience using vocabulary from at least three of the senses in this unit.
Explain the difference between two taste or smell words from this unit and give an example of each.

chapters.title: What your body does
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for bodily actions and reactions, grouped by parts of the body and common situations.
points: Body-action verbs include cough, sneeze, yawn, sigh, chew, lick, blink, wink, and stretch.
Some verbs have related nouns, such as cough and a cough.
Formal and informal alternatives can differ, for example perspire and sweat.
These verbs often describe common physical reactions, habits, and involuntary actions.
Some vocabulary is especially useful for describing facial expressions and breathing.
Body-action words often come in semantic groups rather than isolated items.

examples.english: cough / sneeze / yawn / sigh / snore
chew / lick / swallow / bite
blink / wink / stare / yawn
perspire / sweat / shiver / tremble

pitfalls: Do not assume all body-action verbs form nouns in the same way.
Do not confuse formal alternatives such as perspire with everyday words such as sweat.
Do not ignore the body part or physical situation that usually goes with the verb.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a physical reaction using vocabulary such as cough, sigh, chew, blink, or sweat.
Explain the difference between two body-action verbs from this unit and give an example of each.

chapters.title: Praising and criticising
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for praising and criticising people, actions, and performances. It also includes useful comparison idioms and meanings of critical.
points: Praise vocabulary includes praise, highly praised, brilliant, outstanding, and out of this world.
Criticism vocabulary includes criticise, run down, and idioms such as takes the biscuit.
Comparison expressions include streets ahead and head and shoulders above.
Critical can mean important, severe, or fault-finding depending on context.
Positive and negative judgments often depend on collocations rather than single words.
Idiomatic criticism is often stronger and more informal than direct criticism.

examples.english: praise / highly praised / brilliant / outstanding
criticise / run down / takes the biscuit
streets ahead / head and shoulders above / out of this world
critical issue / critical report / critical of someone

pitfalls: Do not assume critical always has a negative meaning.
Do not use idioms such as takes the biscuit in very formal contexts.
Do not confuse praising language with comparison language; they often overlap but are not identical.

practicePrompts.prompt: Praise or criticise something using vocabulary such as highly praised, outstanding, run down, or critical.
Explain the difference between praise and criticism, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Emotions and moods
chapters.summary: This unit introduces informal fixed expressions for emotions, moods, and physical states. It focuses on figurative language that is especially common in speech and informal writing.
points: Many expressions describe happiness and excitement, such as on cloud nine and in high spirits.
Others describe sadness, anger, or irritation, such as have a long face and throw a wobbly.
Some expressions describe tiredness or physical condition, such as done in or as fit as a fiddle.
These expressions are often figurative and should not be interpreted literally.
They are mainly informal and often add colour or exaggeration.
Mood expressions are often fixed and cannot be changed word by word.

examples.english: on cloud nine / in high spirits
have a long face / throw a wobbly
done in / as fit as a fiddle
over the moon / down in the dumps

pitfalls: Do not interpret these expressions literally.
Do not use very informal mood expressions in formal writing without care.
Do not change the wording of fixed expressions too freely.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe someone’s mood using vocabulary such as on cloud nine, in high spirits, done in, or throw a wobbly.
Explain why fixed expressions for mood should not be translated word for word.

chapters.title: Commenting on problematic situations
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary and idioms for describing problems, confusion, difficult decisions, and possible solutions or turning points.
points: Problem words include chaos, disaster, dilemma, challenge, fix, tight corner, and muddle.
Negative effects can be described with affected, irritating, annoying, disrupted, collapsed, and deprived.
Problem-solving idioms include take the bull by the horns, get your act together, and get to the bottom of things.
Calming or worsening a situation can be described with pour oil on troubled waters and stir things up.
Situation-change idioms include the tide has turned, light at the end of the tunnel, dead end, and turning point.
Some idioms describe avoiding action, such as take a back seat or sweep something under the carpet.

examples.english: chaos / disaster / dilemma / challenge / fix / muddle
disrupted / collapse / deprivation / heartbreak / lack
take the bull by the horns / get your act together / get to the bottom of things
stir things up / pour oil on troubled waters / light at the end of the tunnel

pitfalls: Do not confuse challenge with a purely negative problem word; it often has a more positive slant.
Do not use idioms such as sweep under the carpet without understanding their figurative meaning.
Do not mix up words for confusion, damage, and decision-making; they describe different kinds of difficulty.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a difficult situation using vocabulary such as dilemma, chaos, challenge, or dead end.
Explain how someone could respond to a problem using at least two idioms from this unit.

chapters.title: Number, quantity, degree and intensity
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for number, quantity, degree, and intensity. It also highlights important differences between countable and uncountable patterns and between formal and informal quantity expressions.
points: Number is used with countable nouns, while amount is used with uncountable nouns.
Quantity adjectives include minute, considerable, substantial, excessive, vast, and enormous.
Informal quantity expressions include dozens of, scores of, heaps of, loads of, and tons of.
Intensifiers such as utterly, totally, and wholly combine with particular nouns and adjectives.
Verb agreement with expressions like loads of or tons of depends on the following noun.
Degree and intensity are often expressed through fixed collocations rather than freely combined words.

examples.english: number of students / amount of money
minute amount / considerable number / substantial quantity / excessive amount
dozens of nails / scores of people / heaps of time / tons of food
utter chaos / utterly confused / total disbelief / wholly dependent

pitfalls: Do not use number with uncountable nouns or amount with countable nouns.
Do not ignore the noun after loads of or tons of when choosing verb agreement.
Do not combine utterly, totally, and wholly with just any word; they prefer specific collocations.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a situation involving a large quantity of something, using at least two expressions from this unit.
Explain the difference between number and amount, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Numbers and shapes
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for reading numbers, describing shapes, and talking about basic arithmetic. It includes spoken patterns that are easy to misuse without practice.
points: Numbers are read differently in contexts such as percentages, measurements, telephone numbers, and long figures.
Long numbers are organised with commas for thousands and spoken in larger units.
Shape vocabulary includes two-dimensional and three-dimensional forms together with their related adjectives.
Arithmetic language covers plus, minus, multiplied by, and divided by.
Spoken number patterns often depend on conventional phrasing, especially for telephone numbers.
Shape words are often paired with adjectives such as rectangular, spherical, and triangular.

examples.english: twenty-eight per cent / ten metres by twelve metres
one million, six hundred and twenty-three thousand
oh one double two three
rectangle / rectangular / sphere / spherical / triangle / triangular

pitfalls: Do not confuse decimal points and commas in number reading.
Do not read telephone numbers like ordinary long numbers.
Do not mix a shape noun with the wrong adjective form.

practicePrompts.prompt: Explain how numbers are read in two different contexts from this unit, such as percentages, measurements, or telephone numbers.
Describe several shapes using both the noun and adjective forms from this unit.

chapters.title: Time
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for periods of time, stages, timing expressions, and repeated events. It also clarifies some common mistakes such as on time versus in time.
points: Time vocabulary includes age, era, spell, phase, stage, and period.
Some expressions refer to historical periods, such as the Ice Age and the Middle Ages.
Time phrases include from time to time, at times, time and time again, on time, and in time.
Different words describe different lengths and types of time span.
Time expressions often depend on fixed phrase patterns rather than literal meaning alone.
Repetition and timing are commonly expressed through idiomatic phrases.

examples.english: Ice Age / Middle Ages / a new era / a difficult phase / a spell in hospital
from time to time / at times / time and time again
on time / in time
for the time being / ahead of time / over time

pitfalls: Do not confuse on time with in time.
Do not use spell, phase, and stage as if they always meant the same kind of period.
Do not translate repeated time idioms word for word from another language.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a period in life or history using vocabulary such as era, phase, spell, or stage.
Explain the difference between on time and in time, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Distances and dimensions
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for width, height, depth, length, and related spatial qualities. It also distinguishes between near-synonyms such as wide and broad or tall and high.
points: Wide is usually preferred for physical spaces and objects, while broad often applies to more abstract or general areas.
Tall is typically used for people and narrow high objects, while high is used more generally.
Deep and shallow can describe both physical depth and figurative intensity.
Dimension vocabulary also includes lengthen, shorten, expand, contract, and heighten.
Some spatial words form important figurative expressions, such as broad-minded and narrow-minded.
Dimension vocabulary often relies on contrast pairs.

examples.english: wide road / broad subject / tall building / high wall
deep water / shallow end / deep sadness
lengthen / shorten / expand / contract / heighten
broad-minded / narrow-minded

pitfalls: Do not use tall for every high object.
Do not confuse broad with wide in figurative expressions.
Do not forget that some depth words are common in emotional as well as physical contexts.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a place or object using vocabulary such as wide, broad, tall, high, deep, or shallow.
Explain the difference between two dimension words from this unit and give an example of each.

chapters.title: Obligation, need, possibility and probability
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for obligation, necessity, possibility, and probability. It focuses on differences in strength, formality, and common collocations.
points: Obligation vocabulary includes liable, obliged, duty, and responsibility.
Need and necessity vocabulary includes need, necessity, and absolute necessity.
Probability vocabulary includes probable, likely, unlikely, inevitable, and doubtful.
Chance and possibility overlap, but they differ in tone and formality.
These words are often used in formal explanations, rules, and predictions.
Grammar and preposition patterns are important in this topic.

examples.english: liable for / obliged to / under an obligation
a need for / an absolute necessity
probable / likely / inevitable / doubtful
possibility / chance / probability / certainty

pitfalls: Do not ignore the preposition in phrases such as a need for.
Do not treat chance, possibility, and probability as exact synonyms.
Do not miss the formal tone of words such as obliged or liable.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a situation involving obligation or necessity using vocabulary from this unit.
Explain the difference between possibility, probability, and certainty, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Sound and light
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for sounds, noise, darkness, brightness, and the emotional associations of light and sound words.
points: Sound and noise can both be countable or uncountable, but racket is strongly negative.
Specific sound words include bang, chime, rustle, roar, whistle, and crack.
Darkness and brightness vocabulary includes gloomy, dim, sombre, beam, sparkle, and glow.
Some sound and light words can also describe mood or expression.
These words often work in vivid descriptive writing as well as everyday speech.
Many sound and light words function as both nouns and verbs.

examples.english: sound / noise / racket / strange sounds / roar / rustle
gloomy / dim / sombre / beam of light / sparkle / glow
face glowing with pleasure / sombre expression
chime / bang / whistle / roar
