pitfalls: Do not treat sound, noise, and racket as interchangeable.
Do not assume darkness words are always literal; they may describe mood.
Do not forget that some sound words can be used as verbs as well as nouns.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a place using vocabulary such as racket, roar, dim, sparkle, or beam of light.
Explain the difference between sound, noise, and racket, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Possession and giving
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for ownership, possessions, property, and giving things to other people or institutions.
points: Possessions and belongings are related but not identical; belongings usually refers to smaller portable items.
Property, estate, owner, proprietor, and landlord describe different kinds of ownership.
Giving vocabulary includes donate, give away, provide, supply, and contribute.
Some words in this topic are more formal, such as proprietor and donate.
Ownership words often depend on the kind of thing owned, such as land, money, or movable objects.
This topic combines everyday and legal or institutional vocabulary.

examples.english: possessions / belongings / property / estate
owner / proprietor / landlord / landlady
donate / give away / provide / supply / contribute
leave an estate / own property / give something away

pitfalls: Do not use possessions and belongings as exact synonyms in every context.
Do not forget the more formal tone of proprietor and donate.
Do not confuse property as a general concept with a single building or site.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe something that belongs to someone using vocabulary such as possessions, belongings, property, or estate.
Explain the difference between donating, giving away, and providing, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Movement and speed
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for movement, direction, and speed. It covers verbs for different kinds of motion and nouns that describe speed or rate.
points: Movement verbs vary according to direction and style, such as drift, swerve, creep, cruise, rush, and shoot.
Different kinds of movement are associated with different objects, vehicles, or situations.
Speed vocabulary includes speed, rate, pace, and velocity.
These nouns often combine with the preposition at.
Movement vocabulary often relies on vivid verbs rather than general words like move.
Small differences in movement verbs can signal different speed or direction.

examples.english: drift / swerve / creep / cruise / rush / shoot
flow / sail / drive / travel / glide
at a speed / at a rate / at a pace / velocity
swerve to avoid / creep along / drift across the sky

pitfalls: Do not use the wrong preposition with speed, rate, or pace.
Do not treat all movement verbs as simple synonyms of move.
Do not ignore the direction or speed implied by a verb such as creep or rush.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe movement in a scene using vocabulary such as drift, swerve, creep, rush, or flow.
Explain the difference between speed, rate, pace, and velocity, using examples from this unit.

chapters.title: Texture, brightness, weight and density
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for texture, brightness, weight, and density. It focuses on how objects feel, look, and behave physically.
points: Texture vocabulary includes smooth, rough, silky, polished, coarse, and sticky.
Brightness vocabulary includes bright, dull, shiny, glossy, sparkling, and dim.
Weight vocabulary includes light, heavy, and comparison patterns such as as light as a feather.
Density vocabulary contrasts dense and sparse.
These words help describe physical qualities with more precision than general adjectives.
Texture and brightness words often overlap with figurative description as well.

examples.english: smooth / rough / silky / polished / coarse / sticky
bright / dull / shiny / glossy / sparkling / dim
as light as a feather / as heavy as lead
dense / sparse / thick / thinly scattered

pitfalls: Do not confuse smooth with silky or bright with shiny.
Do not forget that dense and sparse describe opposite distributions.
Do not choose texture words without considering what kind of surface is being described.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe the physical qualities of an object using vocabulary such as smooth, glossy, dense, bright, or heavy.
Explain the difference between two texture or brightness words from this unit and give an example of each.

chapters.title: Success, failure and difficulty
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for success, failure, and dealing with difficult situations. It covers verbs, nouns, and common collocations for achievement and setback.
points: Success verbs include succeed, manage, achieve, accomplish, attain, realise, and fulfil.
Achievement vocabulary often combines with words such as goal, ambition, target, and agreement.
Failure vocabulary includes go wrong, backfire, falter, fold, go under, and come to nothing.
Difficulty expressions include have difficulty, have trouble, have bother, and find something hard.
Cope means deal successfully with a difficult situation.
Word formation in this area includes success, successful, successfully, achievement, and fulfilment.

examples.english: succeed in / manage to / achieve a goal / fulfil an ambition
go wrong / backfire / falter / fold / go under
have difficulty / have trouble / have bother / cope with
success / successful / successfully / achievement / fulfilment

pitfalls: Do not use difficulties when the singular difficulty is needed in fixed expressions.
Do not use successes when the uncountable success meaning is intended.
Do not treat achieve and accomplish as identical in every collocation.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a successful or unsuccessful plan using vocabulary such as achieve, falter, go wrong, or cope.
Explain the difference between success and difficulty expressions from this unit, using examples.

chapters.title: Time: connecting words and expressions
chapters.summary: This unit introduces words and expressions for linking events in time, including sequence, simultaneity, and later or unspecified stages.
points: Use expressions such as 'prior to', 'previously', 'earlier on', and 'formerly' to refer to something that happened before another event.
Use 'in the meantime' and 'meanwhile' for the period between now and a later event.
Use 'at the very time', 'at the very moment', and 'simultaneously' for events that happen together.
Distinguish between 'during' for part of a period and 'throughout' for the whole period.
Use 'following', 'subsequently', and 'thereafter' to show what happened after an earlier event.
Use phrases such as 'at a later stage' and 'at some point' for non-specific time references.

examples.english: prior to meeting the committee
Previously I'd only been to Rome.
Formerly it was Hedge Lane.
In the meantime / Meanwhile, relax and have a drink.
The two events happened simultaneously.

pitfalls: Do not confuse 'formerly' with 'formally'; 'formerly' refers to an earlier state or name.
Use 'throughout' only when something continues from the beginning to the end of a period.
Choose time linkers carefully for register; some are noticeably more formal in writing.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a recent day using at least three time expressions from this unit.
Write two short examples showing the difference between 'during' and 'throughout'.

chapters.title: Condition
chapters.summary: This unit presents different ways to express condition in English, including formal, informal, and spoken alternatives to 'if'.
points: Alternatives to 'if' exist to express condition, such as 'unless' and 'on condition that'.
'In case of' indicates a potential situation requiring action (e.g., 'In case of fire, dial 112').
'As/so long as' and 'provided/providing' are less formal ways to express condition.
'Supposing' and 'what if' are commonly used in spoken language to explore hypothetical situations.
'-ever' constructions indicate that it doesn't matter which option is chosen (e.g., 'whoever', 'wherever').
The present tense is used in the conditional clause (e.g., 'Take a jacket in case it gets cold').

examples.english: You can't come in unless you have a ticket.
You can borrow the bike on condition that you return it by five o'clock.
In case of fire, dial 112.
You can stay, as long as you don't mind sleeping on the sofa.

pitfalls: Don't confuse 'in case of' with 'in case'.
Avoid using the future tense ('will') in conditional clauses (e.g., incorrect: 'Take a jacket in case it will get cold').
Be mindful of the formality level when choosing conditional phrases ('on condition that' is more formal than 'as long as').

practicePrompts.prompt: Write three short conditional sentences using expressions from this unit instead of 'if'.
Explain the difference between 'in case' and 'in case of' with one example of each.

chapters.title: Cause, reason, purpose and result
chapters.summary: This unit introduces vocabulary for expressing cause, reason, purpose, and result in both neutral and more formal English.
points: Words like 'because,' 'since,' and 'as' are common for expressing cause and reason.
Formal expressions like 'owing to,' 'sparked (off),' 'gave rise to,' 'brought about,' and 'stems from' are frequently used in written English.
The purpose of an action can be expressed with words like 'motives,' 'aim,' 'view,' and 'grounds'.
Expressions like 'as a result,' 'as a consequence,' 'consequently,' and 'ensued' are used to describe results.
Understanding the subtle differences in formality between phrases (e.g., 'result' vs. 'consequence') enhances precision.
Careful use of prepositions is important when using these expressions, as demonstrated in the exercises.

examples.english: The collision was caused by ice on the road.
Owing to the fact that the conditions were icy, the two cars collided.
The rise in prices sparked (off) a lot of political protest.
The new law has brought about great changes in education.
Her reason for not going with us was that she had no money.

pitfalls: Remember to use 'owing to' followed by 'the fact that' when constructing sentences with this phrase.
Be mindful of the level of formality when choosing expressions; some are best suited for written English.
Incorrect prepositions can significantly alter the meaning of sentences using cause and result phrases.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write two short sentences about a recent event using one cause expression and one result expression from this unit.
Choose one formal expression from this unit and explain when it sounds more suitable than a simple word like 'because' or 'result'.

chapters.title: Concession and contrast
chapters.summary: This unit focuses on language for concession and contrast, including verbs, adverbs, and fixed phrases for showing difference or opposition.
points: Concession involves accepting part of an idea before presenting a contrasting argument.
Verbs like 'acknowledge', 'admit', and 'concede' are used to express concession.
Informal phrases like 'That's all well and good' signal contrast.
More formal phrases like 'On the contrary' and 'In fact' indicate direct opposition.
Collocations like 'poles apart' and 'a world of difference' emphasize significant differences.
'On the other hand' presents a contrasting viewpoint.

examples.english: I acknowledge that he has worked hard but it isn't enough.
examples.note: Shows concession - agreeing with a point but then disagreeing.

examples.english: That's all very well, but how are you going to pay us back?
In contrast, the USA still uses many non-metric measurements.
When it comes to politics, Jamie and Isla are poles apart.
examples.note: A collocation used to express a significant difference.

pitfalls: Confusing 'on the other hand' with 'on the contrary' - the former presents a contrasting viewpoint, while the latter states the opposite is true.
Using formal phrases ('concede') in informal situations can sound stiff.
Misunderstanding the nuance of 'that's all very well' which implies a criticism or concern despite initial agreement.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write two short examples that show concession and one that shows direct contrast.
Explain the difference between 'on the other hand' and 'on the contrary' with one example of each.

chapters.title: Addition
chapters.summary: This unit covers words and phrases for adding information, from formal linkers in writing to more informal additions used in speech.
points: Addition words and phrases link sentences or clauses to provide further information.
Formal expressions include 'in addition', 'furthermore', 'moreover', and 'further to'.
Informal expressions include 'what's more', 'plus', 'and so on', 'and so forth', 'into the bargain', and 'to boot'.
Expressions can appear at the beginning, middle, or end of sentences or clauses.
Certain expressions are followed by nouns, noun phrases, or verb + -ing forms.
Context dictates the appropriate choice of addition words and phrases; consider the level of formality.

examples.english: For this job you need a degree. In addition, you need at least two years' experience.
Smartphones are becoming more sophisticated. Furthermore, they are becoming cheaper.
Children should respect their parents. Equally, they should respect their teachers.
We'll have stress... On top of (all) that, we'll have to pay the lawyers' bills.

pitfalls: Be mindful of the formality level when choosing an addition phrase.
Avoid using 'as well as' followed by a clause (e.g., 'As well as she speaks Japanese...'); use a noun phrase instead.
Ensure the grammar aligns with the chosen addition phrase (e.g., some phrases require a noun or noun phrase after them).

practicePrompts.prompt: Rewrite two simple sentence pairs using different addition expressions from this unit.
Group three addition expressions from this unit by formality and explain your choices.

chapters.title: Referring words
chapters.summary: This unit explains how referring words help writers and speakers point back to ideas, problems, topics, and solutions already introduced in a text.
points: Text-referring words derive meaning from their context within a text.
Words like 'problem' and 'solution' organize the argument but don't inherently define the topic.
These words often appear in 'problem-solution' texts, connecting presented issues with potential resolutions.
Understanding these referring words is crucial for grasping the overall meaning and structure of a text.
Families of related words, such as 'situation,' 'problem,' and 'solution,' are often used together with specific prepositions.
Politicians often use referring words to support claims and refer to previous statements or investments.

examples.english: problem / issue / topic / aspect
The problem is getting worse each day.
This was the biggest issue in the election.
That's always an interesting topic.

pitfalls: Assuming a text-referring word's meaning is self-evident without considering the surrounding text.
Ignoring the prepositions commonly used with words like 'situation' and 'problem'.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write a short paragraph about a local issue using at least three referring words from this unit.
Explain why words like 'problem' and 'solution' depend on context for their meaning.

chapters.title: Discourse markers in spoken English
chapters.summary: This unit introduces common discourse markers used in spoken English to organise conversation, manage turn-taking, and comment on what is being said.
points: Discourse markers help organize conversations and signal changes in direction.
They can be used to comment on what is being said, expressing agreement, disagreement, or hesitation.
Common discourse markers include 'well,' 'right,' 'OK,' 'now,' 'so,' and 'fine'.
Some markers introduce suggestions or important points, while others prevent interruptions.
Alphabet letters (a, b, c) are often used to list points in informal speech.
Listening for discourse markers can improve comprehension in relaxed listening situations.

examples.english: Well, near Boston.
Right/OK, let's have a look at exercise 3.
So, where was I? I was telling you about my aunt ...
Still / On the other hand, we can't complain, it was fine all last week.

pitfalls: Overusing discourse markers can make speech sound unnatural.
The meaning of a discourse marker can vary depending on context.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write a short dialogue that includes at least three discourse markers from this unit.
Choose two discourse markers from this unit and explain how they help organise spoken interaction.

chapters.title: Linking words in writing
chapters.summary: This unit focuses on linking words and phrases used in writing to structure lists, move to new topics, add clarifications, and conclude a text.
points: Use 'First/Firstly/First of all', 'Secondly/Thirdly', and 'Finally/Lastly' for lists.
Use 'Turning to...' to introduce a new topic.
'In parenthesis...' is used for side issues not central to the main argument.
Employ phrases like 'In summary/To sum up' or 'In conclusion/To conclude' to finish a text.
'That is to say' is a more formal way of explaining something.
'So to speak' or 'as it were' softens a statement, indicating less precision.

examples.english: Firstly, we must consider ...
Turning to the question of foreign policy ...
In summary, we may state that ...
that is to say
so to speak / as it were

pitfalls: Avoid 'At last' when meaning 'finally'; use 'Lastly' instead.
Be mindful of the formality level when using phrases like 'that is to say'.
Remember that 'say' in 'for example, say...' is common in spoken English.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write a short paragraph that uses a sequence linker, a topic-shift phrase, and a concluding phrase from this unit.
Explain the difference between 'lastly' and 'at last' with one example of each.

chapters.title: Talking and communicating
chapters.summary: This unit introduces idioms and expressions for talking about communication problems, types of talk, and common phrases used in conversations and meetings.
points: "Talking at cross-purposes" means misunderstanding each other without realizing it.
"Getting a word in edgeways" describes the difficulty of speaking when others dominate the conversation.
"Talking down to someone" means speaking to them as if they are inferior.
"Talking shop" refers to discussing work-related topics.
"To give someone a talking to" means to scold or reprimand them.
"To start the ball rolling" means to begin a discussion or activity.

examples.english: talk at cross-purposes
get a word in edgeways
talk down to someone
small talk
start the ball rolling

pitfalls: Confusing 'talking at cross-purposes' with a deliberate disagreement.
Misunderstanding the severity of 'giving someone a talking to' - it's a reprimand.
Using 'small talk' inappropriately in serious situations.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write two short examples using communication idioms from this unit.
Explain the difference between 'small talk' and 'talking shop'.

chapters.title: Suffixes
chapters.summary: This unit introduces common suffixes that help form nouns, adjectives, and verbs and make it easier to recognise word class and meaning.
points: -er /@/ indicates a person who does an activity, while -or often means the same thing.
-er and -ee contrast: '-er' signifies the person doing something, '-ee' the person receiving or experiencing it.
-tion, -sion, and -ion form nouns from verbs.
-ist refers to a person, while -ism refers to a belief or activity.
-ness transforms adjectives into nouns.
-able/-ible indicate something 'can be done'.

examples.english: writer, painter, worker
actor, donor, operator
employer / employee
complication, pollution, reduction
goodness, readiness, forgetfulness

pitfalls: The suffix -or is often spelled differently than the verb it's derived from (e.g., act/actor).
Be aware that adding a suffix can sometimes change the stress in a word.

practicePrompts.prompt: Group four words from this unit by suffix and explain what each suffix tells you about meaning or word class.
Write two short examples showing the difference between '-er' and '-ee' words.

chapters.title: Prefixes
chapters.summary: This unit introduces common prefixes that change meaning, create opposites, and help form new words in English.
points: Prefixes are frequently used to create opposites of adjectives (e.g., fair/unfair).
The prefixes 'in-', 'im-', 'ir-', and 'il-' change form before words beginning with 'm', 'p', 'r', and 'l' respectively (e.g., immature, irregular, illegal).
Prefixes 'un-' and 'dis-' often reverse the action of verbs (e.g., tie/untie, appear/disappear).
Some words with prefixes may use a hyphen, so consulting a dictionary is recommended.
Prefixes can have multiple meanings beyond creating opposites, such as indicating 'inside' or 'into' (e.g., internal, import).

examples.english: fair / unfair
experienced / inexperienced
irregular / illegal / impossible
tie / untie
appear / disappear

pitfalls: The prefix 'in-' doesn't always create a negative meaning.
Spelling changes occur with prefixes like 'in-' (to 'im-', 'ir-', 'il-') depending on the following letter.

practicePrompts.prompt: Group five words from this unit by prefix and explain how the prefix changes the meaning.
Write two short examples showing the difference between a negative prefix and a prefix with another meaning, such as 'in-' in 'internal'.

chapters.title: Roots
chapters.summary: This unit explores common Latin roots in English and shows how they help learners recognise meaning, word families, and more formal vocabulary.
points: Many English verbs are formed from Latin roots.
Words with Latin roots are often considered formal.
The Latin root 'DUC/DUCT' means 'lead'.
The Latin root 'PONE/POSE' means 'place' or 'put'.
The Latin root 'PORT' means 'carry' or 'take'.
Latin roots can be used to form nouns as well as verbs.

examples.english: conduct / induce / reduce
postpone / deposit / compose
import / export / transport
He conducted the orchestra with great vigour.
Britain imports cotton and exports wool.

pitfalls: Be mindful of the formal tone when using verbs with Latin roots.
Pay attention to variations within a root (e.g., DUC vs. DUCT).
Consider the nuanced differences between a verb and its noun form derived from the same root.

practicePrompts.prompt: Choose one root from this unit and list several related words built from it.
Explain why words from Latin roots often sound more formal than simpler alternatives.

chapters.title: Abstract nouns
chapters.summary: This unit focuses on abstract nouns, how they are formed, and how they are used to talk about ideas, qualities, and states rather than physical things.
points: Abstract nouns refer to concepts, feelings, or qualities, not physical objects.
Common suffixes for forming abstract nouns include -ment, -ion, -ness, and -ity.
-ment and -ion are typically derived from verbs, while -ness and -ity are derived from adjectives.
Suffixes like -ship, -dom, -th, and -hood are also used to create abstract nouns.
The definite article ('the') is generally not used before abstract nouns, e.g., 'Love is blind'.
Recognizing abstract nouns helps in understanding more complex vocabulary and sentence structures.

examples.english: advice, happiness, intention, (in)justice are abstract nouns.
achievement, affection, carelessness, activity
boredom, breadth, adulthood
Love is blind.

pitfalls: Avoid using 'the' before abstract nouns (e.g., 'The love is blind' is incorrect).
Be mindful of suffix variations, like -ion sometimes becoming -tion or -sion.
Mixing up abstract nouns with concrete nouns can lead to confusion.

practicePrompts.prompt: Group six abstract nouns from this unit by suffix and explain what each group has in common.
Write two short examples that use abstract nouns correctly without unnecessary articles.

chapters.title: Compound adjectives
chapters.summary: This unit introduces compound adjectives and shows how they are formed, written, and used to describe people, things, and situations.
points: Compound adjectives typically consist of two parts and are often hyphenated.
The second part is often a present or past participle (-ing or -ed).
They can describe personal appearance (e.g., curly-haired), personality (e.g., easy-going), or specific situations (e.g., all-out).
Some compound adjectives behave differently depending on their position in a sentence.
Certain compound adjectives use prepositions or particles (e.g., well-off, hard-up).
The form of compound adjectives can be varied (e.g., curly-haired, long-haired).

examples.english: well-dressed
never-ending
sky-blue
rosy-cheeked
easy-going

pitfalls: Be careful with hyphenation - some compound adjectives use a hyphen before a noun but not after a verb (e.g., a well-dressed man vs. He is always well dressed).
The meaning of a compound adjective is derived from its parts, so understand the individual word meanings.
Informal compound adjectives like 'stuck-up' should be used with caution in formal contexts.

practicePrompts.prompt: Describe a person or place using at least three compound adjectives from this unit.
Explain why some compound adjectives use a hyphen before a noun but not after a linking verb.

chapters.title: Compound nouns 1: noun + noun
chapters.summary: This unit explains how compound nouns formed from noun + noun patterns are written, stressed, and understood in English.
points: A compound noun is formed by combining two or more words to act as a noun.
Compound nouns can be written as two separate words (e.g., car park), with a hyphen (e.g., self-control), or as one word (e.g., earring).
The meaning of a compound noun is usually clear if you understand both words (e.g., tin opener).
Stress in compound nouns often falls on the first word, but can occasionally be on the second.
Compound nouns can be countable, uncountable, or used only in the singular or plural.

examples.english: car park
human being
tin opener
self-control
earring

pitfalls: There are no strict rules for how to write compound nouns; variations exist.
Dictionaries sometimes differ on how to write the same compound noun.
Understanding the individual words doesn't always guarantee understanding the compound noun's meaning.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write four compound nouns from this unit and explain what each one means.
Explain why compound nouns can be difficult to write consistently and how a dictionary helps.

chapters.title: Compound nouns 2: verb + preposition
chapters.summary: This unit covers compound nouns formed from verb + preposition combinations, especially those linked to phrasal verbs and journalistic style.
points: Compound nouns are often formed from phrasal verbs.
These compound nouns frequently have an informal feel, especially in reporting.
The meaning of the compound noun is often related to the original phrasal verb.
The plural form is typically created by adding 's' to the end.
These nouns are common in contexts like journalism, economics, and describing modern life.

examples.english: There was a walkout at the factory. [strike]
Last month saw a tremendous shake-up in personnel. [change]
We're trying to find some new outlets for our products. [places to sell]
There are drawbacks as well as advantages to every situation. [negative aspects]

pitfalls: Be aware that the meaning of the compound noun may be different from the individual verb and preposition.
Don't assume all compound nouns formed from verb + preposition are informal - some can be used in more formal settings.
Remember to add 's' to form the plural, e.g., workouts.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write two short examples using compound nouns from this unit in news or business contexts.
Choose two compound nouns from this unit and explain how they relate to their original phrasal verbs.

chapters.title: Binomials
chapters.summary: This unit introduces binomials, fixed two-word expressions whose order is usually stable and often supported by sound or meaning patterns.
points: Binomials are typically joined by the conjunction 'and'.
The order of words in a binomial is usually fixed.
Binomials can be identified by repetition of sounds or rhyme.
Some binomials consist of near synonyms.
Binomials can be formed using grammar words (e.g., 'here and there').

examples.english: odds and ends
give and take
part and parcel
rack and ruin
peace and quiet

pitfalls: The order of words in binomials is crucial; reversing them changes or destroys the meaning.
Not all pairs of words joined by 'and' are binomials. Consider the context and meaning.
Binomials in one language might not have the same order in another.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write three examples of binomials from this unit and explain why their order should not be changed.
Group binomials from this unit by rhyme, alliteration, or near-synonym meaning.

chapters.title: Abbreviations and acronyms
chapters.summary: This unit explains abbreviations, acronyms, and clippings and shows how they are pronounced and used in different contexts.
points: Abbreviations can be pronounced as individual letters or as complete words (acronyms).
Acronyms are often so familiar that they are no longer considered abbreviations and are written in lowercase.
Clippings are shortened, informal versions of words.
Written abbreviations are frequently used in formal writing, such as letters and invitations.
Stress placement changes when abbreviations are stressed words in a sentence; the stress falls on the last letter.
Many acronyms originate from organizations, but some represent concepts or technologies.

examples.english: CIA / BBC
NATO
laser / radar / scuba / sonar
air con
RIP

pitfalls: Remember that some abbreviations are pronounced letter by letter, while others are pronounced as words (acronyms).
Be aware that some acronyms have become so common that they are no longer written in capital letters.
Don't confuse clippings with more formal terms; use them appropriately in informal situations.

practicePrompts.prompt: Group five items from this unit into abbreviations, acronyms, and clippings.
Explain why some acronyms eventually start to look like ordinary words.

chapters.title: Multi-word expressions
chapters.summary: This unit focuses on multi-word expressions and explains why they should be learned as complete units rather than as separate words.
points: Multi-word expressions often have meanings that are not clear from their individual words.
Always record the complete expression, including grammatical information and collocations.
Informal multi-word expressions should be used cautiously and appropriately.
Multi-word expressions can be grouped by grammar, meaning, or key words.
The grammar of multi-word expressions can vary, and some are more fixed than others.
Observe expressions in context and consult dictionaries with care.

examples.english: to feel under the weather
This tin opener has seen better days.
be over the moon
He's as daft as a brush.
I think we should make a move.

pitfalls: Avoid using informal expressions to simply appear fluent.
Be aware of the potential for misinterpretation if used incorrectly.
Don't assume the meaning of a multi-word expression is obvious from its components.

practicePrompts.prompt: Choose three multi-word expressions from this unit and write a short example for each.
Explain why learners should record the grammar and context of a multi-word expression, not just its meaning.

chapters.title: Words commonly mispronounced
chapters.summary: This unit highlights common pronunciation problems in English, including vowel sounds, silent letters, and stress patterns.
points: English vowel sounds can be represented by many different letters.
Several letters are commonly silent in English words.
The stress pattern in two-syllable words can change depending on whether it's a noun or a verb.
Multi-syllable words often have stress on the third syllable from the end.
Apostrophe, catastrophe, and cupboard are examples of words often mispronounced.
Understanding phonetic transcriptions can aid in correct pronunciation.

examples.english: a in 'cat' / 'about'
b silent in 'comb'
noun-verb stress shift in 'conduct'
stress pattern in 'photograph'
silent letters in 'knight' and 'honest'

pitfalls: The letter 'r' can be silent in some varieties of English.
Vowel letters often have multiple pronunciations.
Silent letters can be easily missed.
Stress patterns can be unpredictable, even for native speakers.

practicePrompts.prompt: List three pronunciation problems from this unit and explain what makes each one difficult.
Write two short examples showing how stress can change between noun and verb forms.

chapters.title: Onomatopoeic words
chapters.summary: This unit introduces onomatopoeic words and sound patterns that help create associations with noise, movement, and intensity in English.
points: Onomatopoeic words mimic sounds.
Animal sounds often inspire onomatopoeic verbs (e.g., 'meow', 'roar').
The prefix 'gr-' can suggest negativity or discomfort.
The prefix 'cl-' often implies sharpness or a metallic sound.
The prefix 'sp-' is frequently associated with liquids or powders.
The prefix 'wh-' suggests air movement.
The suffix '-ash' often indicates sudden, forceful action.

examples.english: lions roar / cows moo / bees buzz
groan / grumble / grumpy / growl
click / clang / clip-clop
splash / spit / spray / sprinkle / spurt
whistle / whizz / wheeze

pitfalls: Be careful not to invent onomatopoeic words; use existing ones.
Consider the connotations of prefixes like 'gr-' - they often imply negative feelings.

practicePrompts.prompt: Group examples from this unit by sound pattern, such as 'gr-', 'cl-', 'sp-', or 'wh-'.
Write two short examples that use onomatopoeic words to describe a noisy scene.

chapters.title: Homophones and homographs
chapters.summary: This unit explains homophones and homographs and shows how spelling, pronunciation, and meaning can vary even when words look or sound alike.
points: Homophones are words that sound alike but have different meanings and spellings.
Homographs are words that are spelled the same but have different meanings, and may be pronounced differently.
Many homophones are commonly confused in writing.
The pronunciation of some homographs changes depending on their meaning.
Checking the pronunciation of unfamiliar words is helpful when encountering homographs.
Misunderstanding homophones and homographs can lead to humorous misunderstandings.

examples.english: row (end of a row) and roe (fish eggs)
row (argument) and row (line of seats)
read (past tense) and read (present tense)
sow (female pig) and sow (plant seeds)

pitfalls: Confusing 'whether' and 'weather'.
Mispronouncing homographs.
Incorrectly using homophones in writing.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write two pairs of homophones and explain the difference in meaning.
Choose one homograph from this unit and show how pronunciation changes with meaning.

chapters.title: Uncountable nouns
chapters.summary: This unit focuses on uncountable nouns and explains how they differ from countable nouns in articles, plurals, and common usage.
points: Countable nouns use 'a/an' and can be made plural (e.g., a hat, two hats).
Uncountable nouns are typically not used with 'a/an' or the plural (e.g., information).
Uncountable nouns often group together based on subject or area (e.g., travel, food).
Examples of uncountable nouns include travel, food, advice, knowledge, wealth, information, luggage, and news.
Incorrect use of articles or pluralization with uncountable nouns is a common mistake.
Some words can be countable or uncountable, depending on the meaning (e.g., 'travel' vs. 'a journey').

examples.english: information (NOT an information or some informations)
Travel is an uncountable noun, e.g. Travel broadens the mind.
She gave me some advice on how to study for the exam.
I have some news for you: Henry is getting married.

pitfalls: Avoid using 'a' or 'an' before uncountable nouns (e.g., NOT 'a news').
Do not pluralize uncountable nouns (e.g., NOT 'informations').
Be careful not to use 'a' or 'an' before uncountable nouns like 'weather' (What terrible weather!).

practicePrompts.prompt: List four uncountable nouns from this unit and write a short example for each.
Explain why forms like 'an information' or 'informations' are incorrect.

chapters.title: Words that only occur in the plural
chapters.summary: This unit introduces words that only occur in the plural and explains how they behave in meaning and grammar.
points: Some nouns for tools and instruments only occur in the plural, such as 'scissors' and 'pliers'.
Some clothing items commonly occur in the plural, such as 'trousers' and 'pyjamas'.
Certain nouns connected with appearance, surroundings, and activities are plural-only, such as 'looks', 'belongings', and 'savings'.
Some academic subjects and games end in -s but take singular verbs, such as 'physics' and 'billiards'.
Some words look plural but are actually singular in grammar, such as 'news'.
Context matters because plural form does not always mean plural agreement.

examples.english: scissors
trousers
The news is good.
The proceeds of the concert are going to the children's fund.
The graphics in this game are amazing!

pitfalls: Don't treat plural-only nouns as if they were plural in number agreement (e.g., 'The news are good' is incorrect).
Avoid mistakenly thinking 'spaghetti' is plural; it's treated as a singular noun.
Remember that words like 'looks' refer to an overall impression and are always plural.

practicePrompts.prompt: List five plural-only nouns from this unit and group them by category, such as tools, clothes, or abstract ideas.
Explain why some words ending in -s, such as 'news' or 'physics', do not behave like ordinary plurals.

chapters.title: Countable and uncountable nouns with different meanings
chapters.summary: This unit explains how some nouns can be countable or uncountable with different meanings depending on context.
points: Countable nouns can be counted (e.g., glasses), while uncountable nouns represent a general substance or idea (e.g., glass).
Some nouns have different meanings depending on whether they are countable or uncountable, like 'fish' (a fish vs. fish).
Certain words can function as both countable and uncountable, such as 'hair,' 'paper,' 'iron,' 'experience,' 'arts,' 'time.'
The use of 'a' or 'an' typically indicates a countable noun, while 'some' is more common with uncountable nouns.
Food items often change meaning based on whether they are countable or uncountable (e.g., 'a chocolate' vs. 'some chocolate').

examples.english: We had fish for dinner.
There's a black hair in my soup.
Did you buy a paper this morning?
Would you like a chocolate?

pitfalls: Don't assume a noun is always countable or uncountable; it can change based on context.
Be aware that some nouns have completely different meanings when used as countable versus uncountable nouns.
Incorrectly using 'a/an' with uncountable nouns is a common mistake.

practicePrompts.prompt: Choose two nouns from this unit and explain how their meaning changes when they are countable or uncountable.
Write two pairs of examples, each showing a countable and an uncountable use of the same noun.

chapters.title: Making uncountable nouns countable
chapters.summary: This unit shows how uncountable nouns can be made countable with expressions such as 'a piece of', 'a bit of', and more specific container or quantity words.
points: Uncountable nouns can become countable with 'a piece of' or 'a bit of' (or their plural forms).
'Bit' is less formal than 'piece' and often used with all types of nouns, while 'piece' is more common with uncountable nouns.
'Bit' suggests a smaller amount than 'piece'.
Specific nouns like 'slice', 'blade', 'lump', and 'article' can also be used to make uncountable nouns countable.
The phrase 'state of' is used with abstract nouns like chaos, tension, or poverty.
Certain phrases, like 'stroke of luck' or 'fit of temper', combine with uncountable nouns.

examples.english: What an attractive piece of furniture!
The police collected bits of information from different sources.
Chopin wrote some wonderful pieces of music.
I need a loaf of bread, a couple of slices of ham...

pitfalls: Avoid saying 'What an attractive furniture!' Use 'piece of furniture' instead.
Be mindful of the formality difference between 'piece' and 'bit'.
'Bit' and 'piece' are not always interchangeable.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write four examples that make uncountable nouns countable using expressions from this unit.
Explain when a more specific word such as 'slice', 'lump', or 'stroke' sounds better than 'piece'.

chapters.title: Collective nouns
chapters.summary: This unit introduces collective nouns for groups of people, animals, and things and explains how verb agreement can vary with them.
points: Collective nouns describe groups of people or things.
Examples include 'flock,' 'herd,' 'shoal,' and 'swarm'.
The verb agreement (singular or plural) depends on the perspective.
Some collective nouns have specific connotations (e.g., 'gang' implies negativity).
Common groupings include those involved in a job/activity (team, crew, company, cast, staff, public)
Certain collective nouns describe groupings of physical features (row, clump, range).

examples.english: a group of people
a flock of sheep
a herd of cows
A team of reporters was/were at the scene of the disaster.

pitfalls: Be mindful of the verb agreement rules: a collective noun can take a singular or plural verb.
Some collective nouns have negative connotations, like 'gang'.
The same collective noun (like 'pack') can have multiple meanings.

practicePrompts.prompt: List four collective nouns from this unit and explain what kinds of groups they describe.
Write one example with a collective noun and explain why a singular or plural verb is possible.

chapters.title: Containers and contents
chapters.summary: This unit covers common container words and typical combinations used for what they hold.
points: A 'container' is something that holds things.
Containers are often made from specific materials, such as cloth, plastic, or metal.
Each container typically holds certain types of contents.
The list includes both common household items and items used for storage or transport.
Several containers have specific uses, like a 'bucket' for water or sand.
Words for containers can be varied, like 'tin' and 'packet'.

examples.english: a bag of sweets
a barrel of oil
a box of chocolates
a jar of jam
a tube of toothpaste

pitfalls: Some containers have multiple possible materials (e.g., a bag can be cloth, paper, or plastic).
The contents listed are typical, not exhaustive; containers can hold other items too.
Be mindful of the different materials used for the same container (e.g., a box can be cardboard or wood).

practicePrompts.prompt: Write five container-and-content combinations from this unit.
Group containers from this unit by typical material, such as glass, plastic, or metal.

chapters.title: Expressions with do and make
chapters.summary: This unit introduces common expressions and phrasal verbs with 'do' and 'make' and highlights the different patterns associated with each verb.
points: Phrasal verbs with 'do' and 'make' exist with varied meanings.
Some phrasal verbs have multiple meanings (e.g., 'do up', 'make up', 'make out').
Combinations with 'do' generally relate to activities, duties, or work.
Combinations with 'make' often result in a finished product or outcome.
It's important to note the correct pairings of 'do' and 'make' to avoid common errors.
Many phrasal verbs describe actions that are intended to compensate for or lessen negative feelings.

examples.english: do without
make for the city centre
make up for the uncomfortable rooms
do my homework / make too many mistakes
make the best of a difficult situation

pitfalls: Avoid saying 'make my homework'. The correct phrase is 'do my homework'.
Be mindful of which verb ('do' or 'make') is appropriate for the activity being described.
Some phrasal verbs have multiple meanings; ensure you understand the context to interpret them correctly.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write two short examples with 'do' expressions and two with 'make' expressions from this unit.
Explain the difference between one common 'do' expression and one common 'make' expression.

chapters.title: Expressions with bring and take
chapters.summary: This unit covers phrasal verbs and fixed expressions with 'bring' and 'take', many of which have meanings that are less literal than the base verbs.
points: Expressions with 'bring' often indicate causing something to happen or introducing something.
Expressions with 'take' frequently involve receiving, dealing with, or changing a situation.
The meaning of 'bring' and 'take' phrases can differ significantly from their literal interpretations.
Some 'bring' expressions refer to raising, improving, or persuading someone.
Certain 'take' expressions relate to adopting a skill, liking someone, or experiencing a feeling.
Understanding context is crucial for interpreting the meaning of these phrasal verbs accurately.

examples.english: bring someone up / bring something about / bring taxes down
bring someone round
take after / take back / take in
take off / take over / take up
bring to light / bring into force / bring into the open

pitfalls: Confusing 'bring' with 'take' can easily lead to misunderstandings.
The formal equivalents provided can help clarify meaning, but not all expressions have direct synonyms.
Some phrases can have multiple meanings depending on the context.
Literal interpretations of 'bring' and 'take' expressions often don't make sense.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write two short examples using 'bring' expressions and two using 'take' expressions from this unit.
Choose one phrasal verb with 'bring' and one with 'take' and explain their meanings in context.

chapters.title: Expressions with get
chapters.summary: This unit introduces common expressions and phrasal verbs with 'get' and shows how the verb changes meaning across many contexts.
points: 'Get' has basic meanings including receive, change position, and change state.
It frequently appears in more specific meanings, like preparing a meal or understanding something.
Phrasal verbs using 'get' add nuanced meanings like 'get across' (communicate), 'get behind with' (fall behind), and 'get over' (recover).
'Get' is used in various expressions, such as 'get together' (informal meeting) and 'get one's own back' (revenge).
Understanding the diverse uses of 'get' is crucial for comprehending spoken English and common idioms.

examples.english: get a sandwich / get a text / get top marks
get home / get better / get dinner
get my point across
get behind with my work / get down to some work
get into university / get-together

pitfalls: The many meanings of 'get' can be confusing, so pay attention to context.
Some 'get' expressions are very informal, and alternatives may be preferred in formal situations.
Be careful not to overuse 'get', as it can make your language sound repetitive.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write two short examples using basic meanings of 'get' and two using phrasal verbs from this unit.
Choose one expression with 'get' from this unit and explain its meaning in context.

chapters.title: Expressions with set and put
chapters.summary: This unit covers common expressions and phrasal verbs with 'set' and 'put' and explains how their meanings shift in context.
points: Phrasal verbs with 'set' often involve starting or establishing something, or reserving resources.
Phrasal verbs with 'put' frequently relate to actions like proposing, submitting, or assembling.
The meanings of 'set' and 'put' can significantly change depending on the accompanying word.
Many expressions with 'set' and 'put' are idiomatic, meaning their literal definition doesn't reflect their actual usage.
Some phrasal verbs, like 'set up' or 'put on', have multiple, distinct meanings.
Understanding the context is crucial for correctly interpreting the meaning of these phrasal verbs.

examples.english: Robert had been setting aside money for her wedding.
She put her own name forward for election.
She agreed to help put together some new flat-pack furniture.
The prisoner will be set free tomorrow.

pitfalls: Don't assume the literal meaning of 'set' and 'put' applies - many are idiomatic.
Be careful to identify whether a phrasal verb is separable or inseparable, as this affects word order.
Some phrasal verbs share similar meanings, making it vital to understand the context.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write two short examples using expressions with 'set' and two using expressions with 'put'.
Choose one phrasal verb with 'set' and one with 'put' and explain how context changes their meaning.

chapters.title: Expressions with come and go
chapters.summary: This unit introduces common expressions with 'come' and 'go' and shows how they are used in literal and more idiomatic ways.
points: Expressions with 'come' often indicate arrival, occurrence, or unexpected discovery.
Expressions with 'go' frequently denote movement, continuation, or completion.
'Come to' frequently indicates reaching a state or agreement.
'Come into' often signifies acquiring something or starting a process.
Many 'go' expressions have multiple meanings, requiring careful consideration of context.
Several idiomatic expressions combine 'come' and 'go' to convey nuanced meanings related to success, activity, or acceptance.

examples.english: come off / come to an end / come into a fortune
go on working / go through / go without saying
come round / come up with
go ahead / go back on / go under
It goes without saying that ...

pitfalls: Be aware that 'go' expressions can have multiple meanings depending on the context.
Distinguish between 'come to' (reaching a state) and 'come into' (acquiring or starting).
Pay attention to the subtle differences in meaning when choosing between similar 'come' and 'go' expressions.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write two short examples using expressions with 'come' and two using expressions with 'go'.
Explain the difference between one 'come' expression and one 'go' expression from this unit.

chapters.title: Expressions with other common verbs
chapters.summary: This unit focuses on expressions with other common verbs such as 'look', 'see', 'run', 'turn', 'let', and 'break'.
points: Verbs like 'look,' 'see,' 'run,' 'turn,' 'let,' and 'break' frequently appear in idiomatic expressions.
The meaning of these expressions often differs significantly from the verb's literal meaning.
Expressions can describe actions, feelings, or situations in a more nuanced way.
Recognizing these expressions improves both comprehension and the ability to express oneself more precisely.
Formal correspondence often uses expressions like 'look forward to hearing from you'.
Pay attention to the prepositions that accompany these verbs, as they significantly alter the meaning.

examples.english: look forward to / look into / look down on
see someone off / see through
run into difficulties / run out of time
turn down / turn out / turn up
break down / break out / break up

pitfalls: Don't assume the literal meaning of a verb when used in an expression.
Be careful with prepositions; changing them can completely change the expression's meaning.
Some expressions have formal or informal connotations; consider the context.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write four short examples using expressions with different common verbs from this unit.
Choose one expression from this unit and explain why its meaning is not obvious from the verb alone.

chapters.title: Formal and informal words 1
chapters.summary: This unit introduces formal and informal vocabulary and shows how word choice changes tone, distance, and politeness.
points: Formality relates to the relationship between the speaker/writer and the audience.
Formal language shows respect, politeness, or distance, while informal language conveys friendliness and closeness.
Words can be arranged on a scale from formal to neutral to informal.
Many formal words have Latin or Greek origins and are often longer than their neutral or informal equivalents.
Formal phrases are common in academic and official contexts.
Formal language is frequently found in notices, instructions, and signs.

examples.english: 'offspring' (formal) vs. 'children' (neutral) vs. 'kids' (informal)
'abode' (formal) vs. 'house' (neutral) vs. 'place' (informal)
'go amiss' (formal) vs. 'go wrong' (neutral) vs. 'go pear-shaped' (informal)
'utilise' (formal) vs. 'use' (neutral)

pitfalls: Avoid using informal language just to appear fluent or clever.
Be mindful of the formality level when translating between languages, as direct equivalents might not exist.
Ensure you understand the meaning of formal words in notices and instructions to accurately convey the information.

practicePrompts.prompt: Rewrite two informal expressions from this unit in a more formal way.
Choose one formal word and one informal word from this unit and explain when each is more suitable.

chapters.title: Formal and informal words 2
chapters.summary: This unit continues the study of formal and informal vocabulary, focusing on clippings, slang, and highly informal everyday words.
points: Informal language often uses monosyllabic words (words with one syllable).
Clippings are shortened forms of words that are generally less formal.
Slang is highly informal and can add color to speech but should be used cautiously.
Slang changes rapidly and can sometimes be offensive.
It's safer to recognize slang passively (understanding it when you hear it) rather than actively (using it yourself).
Shortened words often end in '-y' or '-ie'.

examples.english: The cops have arrived. [police]
The house has two loos. [toilets]
I'll get a cab from the station. [taxi]
What's on telly tonight? [television]

pitfalls: Slang can quickly become outdated.
Using slang inappropriately can be offensive.
Relying on informal language in formal settings can be perceived as unprofessional.

practicePrompts.prompt: Replace four informal words from this unit with more formal equivalents.
Explain why slang is often safer to recognise passively than to use actively.

chapters.title: Similes
chapters.summary: This unit introduces similes and shows how fixed comparisons can add colour, emphasis, and informality to English.
points: Similes are comparisons using 'as...as' or 'like'.
Fixed similes are common phrases that are often informal and can be humorous.
Some similes are negative and might be considered offensive (* indicates these).
Similes can be memorable through their sound patterns or by pairing opposites.
The word 'like' can also introduce comparisons, similar to 'as...as'.
Similes using 'as...as' can often be extended: 'as...as can be'.

examples.english: as blind as a bat
as thin as a rake
as strong as an ox
as white as snow
as mad as a hatter

pitfalls: Be mindful of potentially offensive similes marked with *.
Fixed similes are generally informal; consider your audience.
Understand the literal meaning of some similes to grasp their intended meaning (e.g., 'as sick as a dog').

practicePrompts.prompt: Write three examples using similes from this unit.
Choose one simile from this unit and explain what quality it emphasises.

chapters.title: Proverbs
chapters.summary: This unit introduces common proverbs and explains how they are used to comment on situations, give advice, or express warnings.
points: Proverbs are often used to comment on situations or events.
Many proverbs use animal imagery or metaphors.
Proverbs often offer advice or warnings.
Some proverbs can be paraphrased to explain their meaning.
People often use only part of a proverb, expecting the listener to complete it.

examples.english: Don't count your chickens before they hatch.
Don't put all your eggs in one basket.
Never look a gift-horse in the mouth.
Take care of the pennies and the pounds will take care of themselves.

pitfalls: Proverbs often use figurative language, so understanding them requires interpreting the underlying meaning.
The literal translation of a proverb can be confusing or nonsensical.
Sometimes, only part of a proverb is used, requiring the listener to complete it.

practicePrompts.prompt: Choose two proverbs from this unit and explain the practical advice each one gives.
Write two short situations where one of the proverbs from this unit would be appropriate.

chapters.title: The language of signs and notices
chapters.summary: This unit focuses on the language of signs and notices and shows how official wording often differs from ordinary everyday English.
points: Signs and notices often employ specialized language.
The unit provides 'translations' of signs into more common English.
Notices may state rules and consequences for breaking them.
Some signs provide information about services or locations.
Phrases like 'You can be taken to court' indicate legal action.

examples.english: Get off here for Buckingham Palace.
Under 18s must be accompanied by an adult.
Smoking can seriously damage your health.
Trespassers will be prosecuted.
Out of order / Staff only / Keep off the grass

pitfalls: The language used in signs can be formal and less conversational.
Direct translations of signs can be confusing if they miss the intended meaning.
Understanding legal phrasing, such as 'taken to court,' is important.

practicePrompts.prompt: Rewrite two formal notices from this unit in ordinary everyday English.
Write two short sign or notice messages using the style of this unit.

chapters.title: Headline English
chapters.summary: This unit explains how headline English uses short grammar, dramatic vocabulary, and compressed structures to attract attention.
points: Headlines often omit grammar words like articles and auxiliary verbs to be concise.
The present simple tense is frequently used in headlines.
The infinitive form of a verb can indicate a future event.
Newspapers prefer shorter, more dramatic words.
Headlines may use abbreviations, such as PM for Prime Minister.
Newspapers sometimes incorporate jokes and puns into headlines.

examples.english: EARLY CUT IN INTEREST RATES FORECAST
MAYOR OPENS HOSPITAL
PRESIDENT TO VISIT FLOOD AREAS
PM IN ROW OVER TAX PLANS
Headlines often omit articles and auxiliary verbs.

pitfalls: Be aware that headlines often omit grammar words, which can make them difficult to understand out of context.
The use of dramatic vocabulary can sometimes be misleading if taken literally.
Jokes in headlines can be culturally specific and may be lost on some readers.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write two short newspaper-style headlines using patterns from this unit.
Choose one headline feature from this unit and explain how it makes headlines shorter or more dramatic.

chapters.title: US English
chapters.summary: This unit introduces key differences between US English and British English in spelling, vocabulary, pronunciation, and a few areas of grammar.
points: Pronunciation is a key difference between American and British English.
American English often simplifies spellings (e.g., -our becomes -or, -ise becomes -ize).
Vocabulary varies: 'garbage' (AmE) vs. 'rubbish' (BrE), 'truck' (AmE) vs. 'lorry' (BrE).
Some words have different meanings in American and British English, potentially causing confusion.

examples.english: colour / color ; centre / center ; realise / realize
gas(oline) / petrol ; truck / lorry ; baggage / luggage
sidewalk / pavement ; apartment / flat
parking lot / car park ; flashlight / torch
cookie / biscuit ; French fries / chips

pitfalls: Be aware that 'the second floor' means different floors in American and British English.
The word 'bill' has different meanings, referring to a check in American English and a receipt in British English.
The term 'pants' means trousers in British English and underpants in American English, so context is crucial.
